items.summary: AI is now literally building itself. And according to Anthropic, that means two things.

items.title: Everyone's holding their laptops open
items.author: Matthew Berman
items.summary: People are still walking around with their laptops open like this in 2026. It's because people are running AI agents on their laptops, so they can't close it, which is insane.

items.summary: All right, looks like we're going to have another insane week in AI. Probably the biggest piece of news is that Microsoft is back and they just came out swinging.
items.transcriptSummary: All right, looks like we're going to have another insane week in AI. Probably the biggest piece of news is that Microsoft is back and they just came out swinging.

transcript.text: All right, looks like we're going to have another insane week in AI. Probably the biggest piece of news is that Microsoft is back and they just came out swinging. Tons of new AI models, a completely different approach to developing AI as well as a massive partnership with OpenClaw. OpenClaw is now part of the Windows ecosystem. In other news, Bernie Sanders says that he wants to tax all of the different IPOs from these Frontier AI labs. Just a one-time 50% equity tax when they go public. A wild Sam Alman appears out of nowhere and apparently he's meeting Bernie Sanders tomorrow to talk about this and uh I assume just figure out how to kill the whole thing. If there's one person that can sweet talk their way out of something like this, I think it is Sam Alman. And of course, you know, this battle between kind of the artists community or at least some portion of the artist community and AI. There seems to be a lot of tension, a lot of back and forth. A lot of people in Hollywood are strongly against AI use, but every once in a while, the AI side kind of gets a champion. We've had a lot of musicians that came out in support of AI, a lot of people from Hollywood, and within the last 24 hours, we got kind of a heavy hitter. If I were to ask you who is the greatest Hollywood director of all time, you would probably say Tarantino, right? Obviously, I don't think anybody would argue with that. Other than Tarantino, who is the greatest Hollywood director, it's obviously Martin Scorsesei. Martin Scorsesi is working with Flux to help him storyboard some of the movies to generate some of the shots. They're calling it storyboarding and scene exploration, but really what it is is using AI to produce Hollywood films. Also, apparently Chipotle, the burrito restaurant chain, Chipotle is actually really good. I haven't had any in a while, but it's incredible. But apparently, they had a customer support bot that was driven by a large language model. Well, somebody figured out there was a number of unsecured points and vulnerabilities in this AI chatbot. So, they were basically able to use it to get free inference. They got their own unlimited AI assistant powered by and paid for by burritos. Delicious, delicious Chipotle burritos. But first and foremost, let's talk about Microsoft. Microsoft really surprised me with this one. So, Microsoft, I think, was seen by a lot of people as potentially being kind of the casualty of this AI race. For us, instead of seeing a bunch of little pixels, we kind of tend to project some meaning on it, like, oh, this is the enemy. It's going like this. And then we shoot at it. So it's not just pixels in a screen. We have certain mental models about how stuff works. We're abstracting it away from exactly what's on a screen into some higher level abstraction and then we can model against it to see what would happen next etc. So opus 4.8 at least according to this test is closer to humans in that regard than the previous models. And this is exactly the type of shift that we're looking for in these models. not just more trivia and just knowing the answer based on existing data. The idea that you're able to kind of form better hypotheses and then test it and through persistence understand what's happening. So both Arc AGI 3 and the previous benchmark we looked at which was the deep sui benchmark. I think they are both very positive steps in the right direction. So we're trying to come up with better more interesting benchmarks to test these models. We also want to find ones that are not as easily gamed where the results of those benchmarks haven't leaked out onto the internet and the models were trained against them. So they're just memorizing answers as opposed to actually thinking through it and finding solutions. So for example on the deep suite it uses 113 original tasks as as the problems for the benchmark 91 repositories five programming languages and contaminationfree tasks that were written from scratch. So it starts out with shorter prompts and the solutions require a lot more work a lot more tokens to be solved. And this is actually kind of the sort of idea that I'm using to build my own benchmarks because if you look at Arc AGI 3 and Vending Bench and Deep Suite, notice that kind of the thinking about how we're doing these benchmarks, it's changing. No longer are we just saying what score did the model get. We're thinking more along the lines of what kind of thinking did we force the model to do. we're trying to get it to do this specific type of thinking and designing the correct benchmark. The whole point of it is to kind of elicit that type of thinking. So definitely I'm very happy to see kind of the the evolution of the benchmarks. They are getting more and more useful just as these models are getting smarter and smarter. And finally, there's some rumors swirling around about the potential release of GPT 5.6. So again, when we're talking about rumors, you know, take it with a grain of salt. Nothing is confirmed yet, but a lot of people are saying that they're beginning to see references to GPT 5.6 in the OpenAI codeex backlogs and various other places saying that they're seeing GPT 5.6 and GPT 5.6 Pro as potential handles for the model. This model has not been officially announced, and there are no official benchmarks or posts by OpenAI or anything like that. Although tomorrow and Tuesday they do have a live stream coming up. So potentially that's when we're going to see it. But again, nothing official yet, but the amount of rumors that are floating out there is kind of immense. And according to these rumors, it's going to have a pretty big leap in its coding abilities and its agentic abilities and potentially a larger context window, maybe something like 1.5 million tokens. And so this is kind of an ongoing war between now Enthropic and OpenAI. Basically, they're trying to see who can create the best coding agent that can work across the entire repo. They can debug across files. They can continuously work and build and improve without getting lost. What's really interesting is the cadence at which these models are coming out. GPT 5.5 is released by OpenAI in April. So if they're releasing a GPT 5.6 six in let's say June that really suggest these Frontier model launches. They're not going to be these once a year type of events. They're going to be continuously updating rolling updates. So, this is going to be an exciting week and certainly an exciting month. Let me know what you think about this. Which model do you think is the best? Is it the latest anthropic model or the latest OpenAI model? Is it GPT 5.5 or Opus 4.8 or maybe one of the other Opus models? Let me know in the comments. I'm curious to know what you think. If you made it this far, thank you so much for watching.

items.title: Ghost AI let's AI Agents build disposable worlds
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So, I've been experimenting with building these games slash LLM benchmarks recently. This has been something that's really fascinating to me, seeing how well AIs can learn a new game in real time or have them populate a virtual world and see if they can make sense of it.

This has been something that's really fascinating to me, seeing how well AIs can learn a new game in real time or have them populate a virtual world and see if they can make sense of it. If an agent changes some code and gets something wrong, that's usually easily fixable. You can see which files changed. You can compare the new version to the old one. And if the AI agent just goes off on some random tangent in some weird direction, you can just throw away that version and get back to the old one. You don't lose the whole project. Software teams already work this way. You try your ideas in separate versions before deciding what belongs in the final product. But databases are different. It's your users, your orders, your product catalog, your game economy, your analytics events, your settings, your history, etc. It's all the stuff that makes the application feel real. So, if you tell an agent, go improve this product, the agent doesn't need to just change a button or rewrite a paragraph. It may need to touch the data model to rewrite examples. It may need to try different pricing logic, maybe create test users, maybe make some mistakes. And that's the part that feels dangerous unless each agent gets its own disposable copy of the database. And that's the idea that I want to explore in this video. Code already has a safe way to try different versions. databases need the same kind of workflow, especially when AI agents are doing the work. So, here's an actual project that I've been working on. This is to test how well different large language models can learn. I call it Gravell GPT. Basically, how it works is you have these four gravity wells, aka suns, and you have three little ships. the gravity, inertia, physics, all of that is realistically calculated. The LM is tasked with writing a script that is going to control the bots. It doesn't actually control the bots. It writes the code and the logic for these little bots, these little ships to basically fly around. The goal is for them to not crash into each other, not crash into the suns or get even too close to the suns and be pulled into it. And they get points the longer they can stay within this moving disc. I'll give you an example. So here in blue we have cloud code opus 4.7 and in red we have codeex gpt 5.5 high. Let's click replay and we're going to accelerate it just a little bit. So notice it's controlling the three ships by writing the scripts, how they behave, how close they're allowed to get to the sun, how much fuel they're able to use. And the goal is to stay inside this circle as much as possible without crashing. Each time they stay in a circle, they get a plus one score. And the goal is not to crash into the other ships, into the suns, and also not run out of fuel. The whole thing runs for 200 ticks and whoever has the highest score basically wins. Now, these are pro pilots. They don't start out this way. Here's kind of the big trick about this benchmark. The models aren't just given one try. They're given 30. Each time they write code, it gets played out in that simulation and then a feedback is given to it. Here's what happened to your ships. Here's if you crashed. Here's just everything that happened. And with that information, they're able to rewrite their code. So, as you can see here, if the model is smart, this chart increases. Its score increases over time. That means it's learning from its mistakes and it's improving its code. So, I've tested a lot of these models out since I was using the API. At the time of the testing, which was about a month ago, Claude Opus 4.7 reigned supreme. Everything was going great, and then I noticed a problem. Here's the thing. The first iteration, the ships are flown horly. They crash into each other. They explode. They waste too much gas. They just fly out of this solar system or whatever you want to call it. Here's an example. This is the first attempt of cloud code opus 4.7 and GPT 5.5 high. So, here's its first attempt. Notice something blows up right out of the gate. All of them fly out of bounds. They come back way too high. Barely touch. They're just all over the place. They're wasting too much gas. They're just out of control. This is iteration number one. Now, compare that to when they're like 20 or 30 iterations in. Notice they don't try to follow the circle. They kind of wait and let it come to them. They don't use a lot of gas. They're not blowing up. They're not getting too close to the suns. They're just using small little bursts of gas to try to line themselves up. These are pro pilots that have figured out what they're doing. They've earned their stripes. They've earned their experience points and the score reflects it. They went from getting something like 20 points per round to, in this example, 100 plus. You could see the learning rate happening in real time. It was amazing. I had the AI agents running sometimes through the night just testing stuff out and updating everything, updating the database. But then one morning, I noticed something weird. All the models, including some of the smaller ones, the ones that I would assume wouldn't work as well. They all started doing really well right out of the gate. Some of them would clock in at 300 points by the time it was over, but there wasn't a gradual improvement. They just started out good and they stayed pretty good. What happened was somewhere along the line, one of the AI agents that I was using, it decided to add to some code somewhere or to some instruction that I was using for the API calls. It decided to add a hint. It decided to tell these large language models like, "Oh, by the way, here's a great place to start. Here's some kind of like code to get you started." That code was the best found code from all the experiments that we were running. It completely defeated the whole point of the benchmark. It corrupted the results. It corrupted the leaderboards and it wasted quite a bit of money. One AI agent derping out without me realizing was extremely painful. Now, one of my earlier videos was sponsored by Ghost. They reached out. They wanted to do a sponsorship. Their idea was simple. Create databases for AI agents to be used as almost like scratch pads. Spin up three different databases. Get three different agents working on them. Just keep the one that worked and the other ones you can just throw away in the trash. And after doing that earlier sponsored video, I decided to actually do a full video that's dedicated to Ghost and what they do. This is a sponsored video. Thank you to Ghost for sponsoring this video. But I did want to showcase them because if I knew that this existed just a month ago when I was doing this, my whole life would have been different or at least the amount of frustration that I felt when this whole thing happened, it would have been a lot less. So ghost is postgress built for the way that AI agents actually work. So the important thing to understand here is that Ghost isn't just another place to host your database. The interesting thing here is that Ghost is designed around the command line and the MCP because the person using the database might not be a person at all. It might very well be an AI agent. An agent can create a database. It can inspect what's inside of it. It can run queries. It can fork that database. It can run an experiment on it. Then it can just delete that fork or it can keep it for later review. Ghost also has a very generous free tier, unlimited databases and forks, one terabyte of storage. There's no weight list, and they have hard spending caps, so a forgotten experiment is not going to turn into a surprise bill at the end of the month. So if you're building with cloud code or cursor or codeex or any other agentic coding environment, this is the part that matters. Ghost gives the agent a real Postgress database that the agent can manage through MCP. It's super easy to set up. You install it with one flow. Use this curl command here, type it in, hit enter, and it's ghost time. You can choose to configure the whole thing here. Let's walk through that now. We'll hit enter to confirm. It'll give you a link and authorization code to get authorized. Select which MCP clients you want to install. We're going to be using codeex today. So, we'll select that and hit enter. Or if you prefer, type ghost login and that will get you logged in. And then ghost mcpin install. And that'll take you to this screen where you can select what you're going to be using. Again, I already have a codeex configured and ready to go. But you can do cloud code, cursor, Gemini CLI, Google Anti-gravity, Kirro CLI, VS Code, Windsurf, etc. Everything is spelled out on the website, ghost.build, and there's some simple commands for creating the databases, for listing them out, etc. But we can do all that through codeex or whatever agentic coding tool you're using. So now moving forward when I build stuff like this where AI agents can mess with my databases I make sure the first step is it actually creates multiple ghost databases or Postgress databases and then does the experiments separately. So Codex created two separate ghost databases seated both the same schema but different economy conditions. As I'm creating different experiments, I make sure to tell CodeEx to create several different databases. Here, for example, three and spin up three separate workers. So, three sort of sub aents that each work on their own site with their own database, etc. So, each agent is responsible for their own version of the website with its own database, with its own assets. This, by the way, is my version of AI Village. It's not quite complete, but this time I'm not going to lose a bunch of my efforts because of one AI agent derping out and doing something silly. Here, by the way, is kind of what that's looking like. It's not fully complete yet. There's a number of different villagers, each going about their day. There's a a time. You can play it fast, slow, whatever. And four of them are actually being controlled by different LM models. The big point here is that your AI agent can create databases from the terminal or through MCP. But instead of talking about Ghost as just a database management product, I want to focus a little bit on the bigger shift. What changes when your database can be copied, tested, compared, and thrown away as easily as an early draft? We have gotten used to AI editing code. Not perfectly, not without review, but the basic workflow makes sense. You ask an agent to make changes. It edits the files. You check what changed. Maybe you run tests, maybe you reject it, and maybe you keep it. This works because code is easy to separate into different attempts. But the moment that the agent needs realistic data, things get awkward. If you're building an e-commerce site, the agent needs products, prices, coupons, checkout states, abandoned carts, analytics, maybe customer segments. If you're building a game or something that looks like a game, the agent needs rooms, enemies, loot tables, player progression, play test scores, balance settings, etc. This is not just code. That is the world that the code is operating on. And if every agent is working inside the same database, they start stepping on each other. One agent changes the pricing table, the other changes the product copy. The third changes the analytic events. Now the page looks different, but you don't know which idea triggered it, what caused it. Even worse, you cannot compare the attempts cleanly. You don't have three separate versions. You have one messy versions with three AI agents fingerprints all over it. The ghost mental model is simple. Start with one base database, fork it multiple times, give each agent its own copy, and then let them work in isolation. The code and app stay the same, but the world underneath the app is different. When agent A opens the app, it sees the world it created. When agent B opens the app, it sees its own world. Same interface, different database. That is the core idea. If AI agents are going to be doing real work, database state needs a safe way to branch. This matters because agents are not just calculators. They are each their own search processes. You often don't want to take the first answer. You want several plausible attempts. That is especially true in creative work. If you ask three people to design a game level, you don't expect all three of them to create the same map. The valuable thing here is you can run several attempts, compare them, and just keep the best parts. In a game workflow, you can run play tests against each fork. You could score completion time, difficulty spikes, balance, replay-ability, or whatever matters for that specific game. Then you can keep the best fork or send it to a human for review. The key is that keeping that fork is a decision. The agent doesn't just pour changes into the main database. It works in a separate version. You inspect it. You compare it. Then you decide what moves forward. That is the difference between chaos and experimentation. Chaos is three agents writing into the same world. Experimentation is three agents writing into three separate worlds. Chaos is a ladder sounds great on paper. But in practice, not so much. I think this is the mindset shift. Disposable does not mean careless. Disposable means bounded. Means the agent is allowed to explore because the damage is contained. If the level is bad, delete the fork. If the data structure is wrong, delete the fork. If the agent creates nonsense test data, delete the fork. But if one fork is surprisingly good, now you have something very real to inspect. You can run the app against it. You can compare it to the original. You can ask another agent to critique it. You can run test against it. You can determine if it makes sense to move forward with it. This is what makes this different from just letting an agent randomly change things. The agent gets freedom, creative freedom, but it's inside a safe container. And when the work is done, you either throw that container away or promote it. So, as I've created three different takes on this AI village, they have different names, different layouts, different data. I also wanted these agents to create a unique landing page for each. So, this is version number one. One agent did this with its own database based on one version of the game. This is agent two with version two and agent three with version three. Notice how it actually has a replay of the state of the game within it. So imagine you're in a situation you want to create a few different versions of a website or a landing page, whatever the case may be. You want to test a few different directions. Maybe one version is more direct and practical, one is a little bit more editorial. Today, these AI agents are pretty good at making a surface level page. They can write the copy. They can change the layouts. They can generate all the components. But the real product pages aren't just static HTML. They connect to product cataloges, pricing tables, plans, checkout, copy, analytics, events, etc. So if an agent only changes the surface level of the page, it's working on a fake version of the product. The more useful version is this. Each agent gets its own copy of the product database. The different agents try different approaches. One is the simple comm one. One is the more aggressive one. One has multiple experiments and split tests running within it. None of these are automatically the right answer. It gives each agent the chance to try stuff out. Each agent can change the records behind the page without contaminating the other agents work. Same app, same website shell, different database worlds. And because these different versions and different changes live in separate databases, you can evaluate them cleanly across the board. So this is related to AB testing, but it's not exactly the same thing. AB testing usually happens when you have real versions ready to go and you're ready to send real traffic to them. The agent fork happens earlier. This is the messy creative stage where AI agents are creating possible versions before you decide which one of those has the potential to actually be shown to real users. Most of these will probably never reach and the end stage, the production stage. And that's kind of the point. You want agents to be able to explore versions that are too messy, too speculative. They're just not right for the main database. So the loop is simple. Copy the database. Let the agents explore, score the result, then promote the useful version or delete it. This becomes more important with agents because agents can work in parallel. Notice here in codeex the main agent, the manager agent says launch one asset integration worker per site. These three separate workers, one per site, they worked in parallel. Altogether, it took close to an hour. So, if this was one agent, this would have taken closer to three hours to build it out. But three of them working in parallel. Well, technically four, cuz there's one sort of overseer agent, the main codeex agent that's kind of putting them into production and checking the results at the end of it. A human developer usually works on one version at a time. A team of developers might have several different versions happening at the same time, but each person is still moving at human speed. Agents change that. If you can run 10 agents, the bottleneck is no longer if somebody can type the code that fast. The bottleneck becomes can you give those agents a safe place to work and can you rate and review the output at the end of it? Can you compare what they produce afterwards? For code, we already have a mature workflow for this. For databases, the answers have so far been kind of clunky. You might manually copy a database. You might create a staging environment. You might use a local database. You might try to keep things synced by hand. But that friction usually means that the agent gets one of two bad options. It either gets a fake environment, meaning it's not solving a real problem, or it gets a shared environment, which means it can make a real mess. Ghost is trying to make the correct thing the easy thing. Fork the database and let the agents do the work, then delete or promote the result. In practical terms, Ghost is a managed Postgress with an interface built for agents. You still get the normal Postgress databases, but the workflow is built around the command line and MCP instead of a big visual dashboard. That means that the coding agent can manage more of the database life cycle itself. It can create a new database. It can fork an existing one. It can inspect the structure of the database. It can run SQL and it can delete the scratch database when it's done. And because it's Postgress, it's not a toy database that just exists for demos. The point is to give agents a real relational database, but with the branching behaviors that agent workflows need. And the other big practical piece of this is cost control. Ghost offers 1 TBTE of free storage, unlimited databases and forkworks, and hard spending caps. That matters because agent workflows can get weird fast. If you're going to let agents create infrastructure, you're going to need boundaries. The promise is not let the agent do anything. The promise is let the agent explore within a bounded system. The bigger point is that AI software development is moving from a singleshot generation to parallel exploration. It's not just make me one button. It's try three product strategies. It's design five different onboarding flows or generate 10 different levels, play test them and see which one's the best. These workflows are only clean if the agents have their own isolated state because the moment that multiple agents share a state comparison gets muddy. If two different agents change the database, which one caused the lift and which one broke the data model? Which one changed the price? Which one created the weird edge case? Separate database branches preserve the experiment. This is why I think this is the missing piece for agentic code development. We already know code needs safe versions. The next step is realizing that the world behind code needs its own safe versions as well. If you want to try ghost, go to ghost.build. There's no wait list. You can install the CLI and connect it to your agent with this one command. Type in this curl command, then ghost login, then ghost mcp install. It's as simple as that. You get 1 TBTE free unlimited database and forks and hard spending caps so agent experiments stay bounded. For me, the interesting part is not just that the agent can create its own database. is that the agent can create its own copy of the world. Try something real and interesting inside of it and then you can decide what survives. This is the workflow that I think we're going to be using a lot more of as agents become less of an autocomplete and more like parallel builders. Code has safe versions. Database state needs safe versions and Ghost is building directly for that. Thanks to Ghost for sponsoring this video. Link is in the description. If you made it this far, thank you so much for watching.

And this is the defining factor that I think is going to be so important in a new era is that experts become more important. Yes, anyone can create YouTube thumbnails, but somebody with a very sharp design eye and understanding how they work can create better ones. Hopefully, anyone can produce code and create software, but you really need that engineering mind to understand how to build something properly, how to test it to make sure that it's quality. Anyone can draft a newsletter or a book or a blog post, but you really need great writers and editors to make it into something good, something unique. So, what this means is that automation isn't magic dust that you just sprinkle over work to make it disappear. It just becomes the operating system on which you need still maintenance, evals, right? You need to figure out what's good, what isn't. You need the right sets of permissions. What can the AI automate? What it can't? What is it allowed to do? What it isn't? You need the review cues to make sure that the process keeps going. You need instruction files. A lot of my time, not a lot, but a significant amount of time is spent writing very in-depth, very involved instruction files. And at the end of the day, you do still need human ownership. You need somebody to say, "Hey, like I made sure this is good. This is unique. I put my own flavor on it that it's not just slop. The point is AI can automate the middle of the task. We assume is we still decide what tasks matter, what outcomes we're trying to achieve. And at the end, we need to decide which one of the versions is better, if it's exactly the outcome that we were looking for, and then repeat that process again. So now that we're here, what comes next? Humans still control the beginning and the end. So that means that the work that we do, the jobs, they they change. They don't go away, but they they evolve. The amount of money you're going to be getting paid in the past might have been for the stuff that you you did, for the lines that you typed, but moving forward, companies will be paying humans for the decisions that they make, for them supervising what gets done. They need to integrate it into the company. they need to differentiate it from all the other stuff that's produced and at the end of the day they need to take ownership for that stuff. So this means that this idea of a permanent underclass might not exist. This is not what we're heading towards at least when it comes to jobs. We may yet still have a permanent underclass but not for people in and for workers but rather for companies. That's because AI doesn't help every business equally. There are a lot of companies that their moat, how they make their money, is based on this idea that used to be difficult to produce certain things. The competence was very expensive. We've already seen Anthropic publish a few things that were open- source that crashed the stock valuations of certain companies. So if you had some specific set of code that dealt with some legal aspect for example because it was hard to develop that you needed somebody that was competent in writing code. You need somebody that was competent in the legal profession. They could charge a lot of money per seat for certain companies like legal companies to buy that software then charge and per se to have access to that software. With AI that moat is basically gone overnight. But companies that have the right data workflow, these new people that are able to use AI, they might win bigger and bigger shares of the market. And in fact, AI powered companies might expand and wipe out other companies. This is still kind of unclear. We don't know exactly where this is going to be going. I don't tend to know how this is going to play out, but that might be kind of the next thing to watch. How disproportionate will AI be for companies? So, I think the takeaway at the end of the day is that so far it's looking like maybe the whole jobs going away thing was overstated. Again, we're not saying that this is the new future. We know it for sure. We're saying that the hypothesis changed. So, over time, we'll see more and more data that either proves or disproves this. But for the time being, it might be that the jobs are evolving but not going away. And in terms of how AI will affect the companies, the big question here is is it that AI will raise every company's productivity, you know, let's say 1% or 5% or is it that it's going to take the top 5% of companies, raise their productivity 20%, but not necessarily everyone else. The difference between those two scenarios will make a drastic impact on how business is done, on what the stock market looks like. But the big point here is I think for a lot of us our intuitions about what AI is going to do were off in terms of specifically how it's going to affect jobs. Now I think the best advice that I or maybe anyone can give people that are worried about their jobs is this. Use all the new models as they come out and understand where you fit in into that work process. Think of yourself as managing the inputs and the outputs. So on the input side, what are you prompted with? What context do you give it? Like what model do you use? Everything that you can decide on the input side to improve how well the model works and then on the output decide which of the different versions you're going to use. If the job it completed, was it good? Was it not good? The people that figure out how to do those sides of the equation, I think they're going to do very, very well. But just like we all had to learn to type and interact with computers, I think doing this and working with AI agents will just be table stakes. Also, it does seem like different job titles will maybe either go away or at least become a little bit more diffused, a little bit more vague because again the competence is going away. Like you might have been really good at this particular thing and that was your job title. Now, more and more we're going to be doing a lot of different things across job titles and be more like project managers. Maybe that's a better term for it. So, I'll leave you with this. I think you're going to be seeing a lot of headlines saying that AI companies did a full 180 and kind of flipped on what they were saying before. And some of those publications will probably try to paint this as a preipo change of, you know, marketing and PR, etc. I don't think that's a fair claim. And that's not a fair framing because a lot of us that use this technology on a daily basis, we're all noticing a similar shift. Each new iteration of the model is allowing us to automate more. It does more for us. We're more and more amazed at the stuff that it's able to do. It gives us more productivity, more output, but it doesn't reduce the amount of time that we have to work. If this trend continues, and this is important, we have to realize that it kind of negates the two biggest AI risks that we've ever talked about. First of all, if we have to manage it and make sure the outputs are right, then that whole thing about it killing everybody, well, that's probably not going to happen. Not without a human being sitting there and going like, "Okay, let's make a plan for how to kill everybody." And then on the other side going, "Well, I don't know if that's going to work. Let's get some better ideas. Let's uh let's see if we can improve the process. Which by the way, that idea of humans using AI to do something nefarious, that can always happen. Any technology can be abused. But the specific X risk from rogue AI as it in developing self-awareness and then going all Skynet Terminator 2 style. I always say Terminator 2. I've never seen Terminator 1. I I just saw the second one. To me, that's the Terminator movie. I I don't even know what happens in the first one. But the point is, if this pattern holds, then those two big risks go away. There's no X-risk from rogue AI specifically, and there's no AI job apocalypse. There's a massive explosion in work productivity. How we do work changes. The internet changes. Tons of things still get disrupted, but the two biggest fears might be a thing of the past. Let me know what you think about this. Do you agree with this hypothesis? Do you believe what Sam and Daario are saying or do you think it's just marketing for their IPO? Let me know in the comments. If you made this far, thank you so much for watching. My name is Wes Roth. will see you in the next

items.title: Claude, The Pope, and AGI
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.
items.transcriptSummary: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it.

transcript.text: All right, so there's some big news today as Anthropic joins forces with the Vatican to talk about AI on a global scale. So let's talk about it. It's Opus Day and Dario Amade and everything that goes with it. So this doesn't seem like a photo op or some brief meeting. This seems a little bit more involved. So, Anthropic is of course one of the major frontier AI labs led by Daario Amade and others and it is in many ways different from the other labs. They do have a lot of self-imposed moral standards. They are trying to solve this AI alignment thing, making sure that the super intelligence that they build is well behaved and does not harm humanity. Anthropic recently had massive problems with the Department of War based on some of the red lines that it was not willing to walk away from. Spying on US citizens and autonomous weapons powered by AI were the crux of the issue. Anthropic refused to budge and Google and OpenAI became the defense contractors and Anthropic was designated a supply chain risk. But they've been scoring a lot of reputational points recently, both against their push back on the Department of War and recently getting Andre Karpathy to join the team who is very well respected in the industry by pretty much everybody both the academics and the researchers and the engineers and the AI enthusiasts. He is highly regarded and this is yet another big victory in terms of whether you want to call it PR or just winning goodwill amongst the people. I mean this is it. The Catholic Church has something like one and a half billion members and the church announced an ethical collaboration with Anthropic. I mean think about that for just a second. This is the world's first AI company to literally receive the blessings of the pope. Did you expect to hear that headline in your lifetime? By the way, really fast, I've been saying this on this channel probably 100 plus times now since we started and that is AI will intersect with every part of everyone's lives. It's not just a coding tool. It's not just for writing emails. We've seen it hit jobs and art and movies and finance and cyber security. And now it's kind of intersecting with one of the biggest religions on the planet. And again, this is just scratching the surface. Okay, so there's kind of two main players here that we're going to be talking about. One is Christopher Ola. So he is an anthropic co-founder. So he's one of the main people at Anthropic and he's one of the most important figures in AI interpretability. So this isn't a sales guy. This isn't an executive. This guy is a researcher. He's working so that we can understand AI and how it thinks, what makes it tick, how it behaves. He said a few things during the ceremony, during the interview, press conference, whatever you want to call it, that are already being wildly wildly misinterpreted. And the other person is, of course, the pope. So he's kind of representing the views of the church. And between the two of them, there's like three major things that I think this is about. The first one is an understanding that AI will displace jobs. So neither one of those people are mincing their words. They're saying this is coming. Their concern is with humanity, with the people, with the global poor. What do we need to do now to start thinking about the transition? On this channel, by the way, we talk about that quite a bit. As AI automation takes over, the demand for human labor will decrease. I don't know what time scale it's going to happen on over a year, 5 years, 10 years, 50 years, but as a lot of people in the AI field have said like this system will get disrupted. this idea of you contributing mental or physical labor in exchange for resources and being able to interact in in society in the economy that's going away or at least it will change. It will be greatly disrupted and we need some sort of a model some sort of a plan for what happens next. So the fact that more and more people are taking this seriously and talking about this is great. Google DeepMind recently hired a chief AGI economist or something like that. basically someone that's going to start looking into how to navigate that transition. The fact that the Vatican is talking about it is good, too. I don't know what their plans are, but the fact that more people are saying it and more people are hearing this message and we'll need to start kind of wrestling with it and trying to at least understand it, I think overall that's a good thing. The second big message that they're talking about is that these AI labs, well, they're not to just be trusted by default, so to speak. As the Pope said, AI is not neutral. The people that are building it, designing it, they're kind of infusing their own ideas into it. It's important that other people participate in the shaping of AI. And the third is kind of talking about what AI is, how it's a black box that we don't fully understand. And we need to be able to detect dangerous patterns as this thing gets better and better and smarter before it can cause realworld harm. And let's start there because I already see the most confusion out there about this and the thing that Christopher Ola was saying in one of the messages. He talked about the AI models having something that appears to us to be something like emotions or introspection or other things that are similar to what humans possess. And this is where a lot of the conversation breaks down again because words can be a little bit vague. They can have a very different meaning to different people. We covered a lot of research on this channel coming out about anthropic about how these models behave. We've read their paper about introspection about Claude and the other AI models have something that can be called introspection. We've read a paper from Enthropic that covers how these models can have functional feelings. But when a lot of people out there in the world when they hear it and don't really understand those concepts, they will kind of scoff at it. They're going to say, "Oh, the people at Anthropic think that Claude has feelings and we need to be nice to it." Kind of making fun of that idea. Here's the thing. Nowhere in those research papers that we've read, to my knowledge do any of the people at Anthropic, no one's saying that these AI models have some sort of a subjective experience. We're not talking about pain or pleasure, happy or sad. No one's saying this is a living thing. I think a more accurate way of saying it is as we scale up these neural networks that are by the way kind of mimicking the human brain a little bit. The neural network in in our head is what we've tried to recreate digitally with these neural networks. And as we scale them up and task them doing more and more complex things and expose them to more data, certain things emerge. And some of these things are very unique and alien and different from how we've evolved and how the human brain works. But some things seemingly are similar. So if you've ever had an experience where you keep thinking about something and you go, why do I keep thinking about that? So you start thinking about why you're thinking about that thing. You're kind of introspecting. You're thinking about your own thinking processes and trying to understand where they're coming from. Well, it seems that Claude does have something different. It's able to introspect. It's able to notice when certain thoughts and ideas are being injected and it's able to kind of catch them and think about them. Now, I'm sure no one taught you to do that. You just notice that ability one day. And the same thing is true for Claude. They didn't train it to be able to do that. This skill or feature emerged at some point. And it's the same thing with these functional feelings that these models have. So it's a way to create a state that helps them better predict what words to use next. And those states can map to certain feelings that we might have. So it's not saying that they're having those feelings or subjective experiences. It's saying that they're able to model those things and have some representation about those things. So that's why they call them kind of functional feelings. So this is where Christopher Ola's words I think are being a little bit misinterpreted. His point is that these AI models, they're not engineered like airplanes or bridges. They're grown on this massive amount of human knowledge and text and speech. As he says, AI models are made from us, from our worlds, and they are far more subtle and odd and beautiful than science fiction prepared us for. So, I think that's an important point to understand because he's not saying Claude has feelings. What he's saying is these things are weird, mysterious, unsettling, and answers about those things and how they're going to affect the world and our stance towards them. It can't just come from engineers, right? If I get on an airplane or into a car, I want all the safety features, right? I want engineers to have the last word where those things are concerned. I I I want engineers and people with that mindset to be in charge of that. They're saying with AI, that's not enough. We need more different perspectives to to fully understand this thing and where it's going. And the second big point about what they're talking about is the massive massive job displacement that will be triggered by AI. They're saying that AI displacement is now a moral crisis. If there's a real possibility of AI displacing massive amounts of human jobs, then supporting those displaced workers becomes a moral imperative of historic proportions. Again, this is somebody that's a co-founder of an AI lab, a billionaire. This is not a a union organizer. This is not a politician. This is somebody that's leading the charge and at the same time warning against it. The Pope's framing also supports this. He's asking if AI replaces work, what sort of replaces the dignity and the social function that our trade, our work has served before. Also, who captures all those productivity gains? Who pays for the transition? Like how do we transition? What happens to families? What happens to young people if stable employment just disappears? And is universal basic income, is that enough? Or is there some other portions of jobs and work that provide meaning and other things that we need? And the third big thing that I think they're talking about is this idea that AI labs right now, as they say, hold too much power. Interestingly, the Pope's argument is not anti-technology. It's more pro-humanity or anti-dehumanization. The pope doesn't make a lot of points that AI can help heal and educate and unite and and provide a lot of good things just like at the same time it can cause a lot of harm, automate warfare, it can divide people, manipulate, etc. He makes the big point that AI is not neutral. The people that are building it, financing it, promoting it, like all those people have an impact and an effect on how this AI acts, behaves, what ideals it supports. Can you imagine if the church comes out as pro- opensource, like they give their full blessing to open source AI? Wouldn't that be interesting? It does seem fitting, doesn't it? Let me know in the comments if you agree with that. Would organizations like the Catholic Church, would they be pro- open-source AI and open- source software? Now, at the same time, they caution that these private companies, these AI companies, a lot of them about to go public, that they hold state level power. If you think about it, a lot of these companies can now affect markets. They can affect war. We've seen that with anthropic, you know, front and center. How well they're able to hold the line against someone like the department of war. They affect education, geopolitics, and there's really just a handful of them at the center of this. So, the pope is saying regulation is necessary, but it's not enough. This is one of the sort of concerns that I personally have and I apologize for the tangent, but there's a lot of people that are just saying we need more regulation, we need more weigh in, we need more this. It's like this idea that if we just have more people yelling their opinions, then somehow everything's going to get better. Just adding more red tape and bureaucracy doesn't necessarily just make everything better. So right now this is the the first day that we got access to a lot of the statements and the publications that they did. I am very happy about how they're talking about thinking about AI job displacement. But this idea of regulation and insight and oversight and stuff like that, it still feels a little bit vague to me. I I hope they flesh it out a little bit better because there's tons of stuff that you can say that sounds really nice and is hard to argue with. You can say everyone should have a choice and everyone should weigh in. It should work for everybody. It should be democratic and and yes, all those things are good, but they're a little bit meaningless. They're a little bit too vague. What exactly are we talking about? The next big point is the fact that work isn't just about income. So having a job isn't just paycheck. It's an identity. It's belonging. It's dignity. It's family stability. By the way, this I think is a very important thing. This is my personal belief that we need to separate, right? This idea of getting resources in exchange for labor from the meaning and dignity and political power because workers do have political power in their ability to refuse to work, shutting down key industries. Certainly, we've seen examples of nations where they have enough natural resources, where they're not as reliant on the population as some other nations. And there does seem to be a pattern, not always, but in some cases, of maybe those governments not being the nicest to their people. So, if you're not afraid of the population refusing to work, you you might overstep your bounds a little bit. Whereas if your nation requires the workforce to work and to play nice with you, you're going to be forced to be nicer. So whatever model we're transitioning to or at least we're thinking about it has to take those things into account, right? So not just the paycheck, the resources needed to survive, family stability, etc. We also need to think about what happens when the powers that be don't need workers cuz we've been replaced with AI and robots. One thing I've always said is I don't think UBI is going to help if it's made like a just a cash payment, like a monthly cash payment, because there's way too many tricks that you can play to mess with people, right? You can take it away if they don't support you, if they don't say the right things, or you can print more money, dilute the money supply so that you know a,000 bucks a month or whatever it is becomes meaningless. Whatever new system we have, it has to be equity based. If you're a unique human being, you have to have some ownership that cannot be ever taken away from you. You also can't take out loans. You can't go in debt against this thing. Like it has to be just protected from anything like you have this equity, this ownership in the nation that you live in or whatever other organization sort of makes sense and it can't be taken away from you and it gives you some share of the profits. One thing that I'm really in favor of is having it somehow be based in compute. That's the currency, the money of modern times. We need compute to run these systems. So if this gets scaled up, everybody loses their jobs. But every single individual has some sort of share of the global compute pie and get some sort of dividends based on how well these robots and AIs are working. Then everyone's incentives are aligned. It's okay if robots do the job and we get a cut of those productivity gains. There is a way to make this work and make it good for everybody. We just have to kind of model it out and figure out what it is. All right. So this is something that I think we need to watch carefully because this is of course a very large powerful influential religion organization. It's a global force. One and a half billion members. Members or followers, whatever word you want to use there. I think how this conversation continues is going to be very important. How the world deals with AI. So far, a lot of it seems to be very positive. We're talking about how to make the transition if and when AI starts displacing jobs. We're beginning to talk about some of the weirder aspects of how we're growing these AI models. And I know there's going to be a lot of push back on it. Sure, some people are going to be framing this as anthropic, trying to prove that Claude has a soul. But the point is that the Vatican is stepping in at the exact moment when these AI labs are beginning to kind of grapple with the concept that these AI models aren't just tools. That there are some humanlike cognitive processes. And of course there's going to be people in the future that will push towards personhood and these things maybe have some moral considerations. And I think Anthropic's point is that it is right now a little bit vague. We don't have proof one way or another. We are just beginning to get insights into this and how the conversation develops is going to be important. So with that said, let me know what you think about this whole thing. Religion has spent centuries building moral constitutions for human behavior. Do you agree that they should also build constitutions for AI, ethical and moral behaviors? Do you think that has a place in conversation? Maybe maybe you don't. Maybe you're you're scared that this is happening and it might influence everything in a negative way. Let me know. I'm very curious to know how people are perceiving this. Make sure you're subscribed. Please hit thumbs up. It helps more than you know. My name is Wes Roth and I will see you in the next

items.title: The REAL Reason Andrej Karpathy Joined Anthropic
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story.
items.transcriptSummary: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story.

transcript.text: So, in case you haven't heard about this yet, Andre Karpathy just joined Anthropic. Now, this is presented as a talent wars story. Getting Carpathy to join your lab is a big big win for a number of different reasons. He's not just a top tier researcher. He's an icon, if you will. Meaning, legitimately, he's a cultural influence at this point. He can explain hard technical ideas and make it kind of obvious, make it easy to understand. He's also, at least for the time being, shutting down his AI education company. But I think there's a whole other story developing here that people are missing. And it has to do with what Andre has been working on in his time away from OpenAI. And this same thing also seems like it's causing a pretty big rift in opinions from the heads of these major AI labs with Google on one side and Anthropic on the other. So, first and foremost, what actually happened? Andre Karpathy announced that he's joining Anthropic on May 19th, 2026. His new role is to be part of Anthropic's pre-training team. He reports to Nick Joseph, Anthropic's head of pre-training. Anthropic told multiple outlets exactly what Karpathy will be working on. He will build a new team focused on, this is the important part, using Claude to accelerate pre-training research. And just on the off chance, you don't know Claude isn't just a guy, it's the AI. I didn't mean for that to rhyme. What I'm saying is Claude is anthropics AI. Karpathy will be using AI to accelerate pre-training research. So he's going to use AI to improve future versions of AI. Since Andre left OpenAI, he's been releasing a number of opensource projects in the AI space. They were very interesting. But if I had to pick one that really stands out, it's Auto Researcher. Auto Researcher uses a very small large language model to do machine learning research. It's kind of those kits that you get kids for them to start learning engineering skills. You know what I mean? Like you you actually use electricity or whatever physics or whatever you're trying to get them to learn about, but it's kind of like scaled down and simplified. That's kind of what auto researcher is. It allows all of us to start dipping our feet into doing automated machine learning research. Now, it's a lot bigger than that because as Karpathy himself said it, a lot of these little wins that you can get from your home computer, from these small language models running on your home computer, those can translate and scale up to actual training for actual large language models. I am like 99% sure I put out a video about the auto researcher, but in case you haven't heard of it, Fortune magazine actually described it in a very interesting way, in a very simple to understand way. They called it the Carpathy loop and that phrasing is actually very very useful because it captures that recursive structure. How it works is AI proposes a change. In the scientific method, this would be called a hypothesis. This is like if we test this, we believe that something might happen. This is something that we want to test. In marketing, it's a split test or an AB test. Online, people say F AFO, whatever you want to call it. It's we're proposing a change and we're going to see what happens when we make that change. So AI proposes a change and step two, AI tests that change. So maybe there's a piece of code that is written a certain way. So the AI says, "Okay, what if we change it to be slightly different?" It changes it and then it tests it. It runs whatever experiments it needs. It gathers the data. Then step three, it evaluates the results. Usually here, you need some sort of a KPI, key performance indicator or or something that you're testing that has to be qualitatively better or worse. Like if you're trying to make a website load faster, right? If you cut down, if you cut down from 1 second to.9 seconds, then it's better. If it starts taking 1.1 seconds to load, it's worse, right? So, you need something that's either better or worse. And then that's step four. If it's better, then you implement the change and then you start that loop all over again. And Carpathy's auto researcher is a very real, very working, but sort of like toy version of this. Make no mistake, it works. It produces results. It just really scaled down. It's a very simple model. You can run it on your home computer, but it's very, very real. Now, not that long ago, we covered a post by Jack Clark, who is the co-founder of Anthropic. This might be the most important kind of kind of strategic context. It was that blog post by Jack Clark. In his post, Jack Clark had this pretty striking claim. He said that he believes that there's a 60 plus% chance that we're going to reach no human involved AI research and development by 2028. Specifically, he said the end of 2028. So, we got about 2 and a half years until AI progress is driven entirely by AI. And again, he gives that a 60% chance. So he's not saying this is guaranteed, but it's probably something we should be preparing for, and it's something that the heads of various AI labs believe in. Now, if you've worked with these high-end engineers, researchers, people with worldclass talent, at least in my experience, it's not like they just go to the highest bidder. I've seen a number of people of that caliber walk away from earning more money because there was another offer on the table where it was more aligned with their vision and perhaps even more importantly it has to be technically interesting to them. So I believe for Andre to have joined Anthropic a number of things had to be true. Number one I'm sure the pay is good but that's not the deciding factor. Number two, Karpathy believes in anthropic, in the team, in their vision, in what they're trying to do. They're aligned about how they see the future. And number three, the technical challenge that was presented to Andre had to be delicious enough to get him out of retirement and out of running his AI education company. like it had to be so good that he needed to go back and get back inside the fray and continue developing AI. I'm sure it wasn't an easy decision. He probably said something along the lines of that every time I thought I was out, you drag me back in or something like that. So for the guy that co-founded OpenAI and made a lot of progress there, for the guy who was a former Tesla AI director and then more recently launched his own Eureka Labs, his AI education company, every video he posted on YouTube gets millions of views. Like he didn't need anything. the quote unquote bait that you have to have to get him in has to be just the most interesting and challenging and impactful project and resources where if he comes in he would be like the final spark that's a unique talent needed to really progress that thing forward. So I think that's the most important takeaway. Carpathy didn't join Anthropic to work on Claude or work on safety. He joined specifically to use Claude to make Claude better to set that flywheel of recursive self-improvement to start it spinning or more accurately I think to accelerate it. If Anthropic wanted a celebrity higher, they would have put him in some flashy general role. They're not. They're putting him in straight into the machinery room. Anthropic is not buying status. items.title: OpenAI just SOLVED MATH....
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: So 7 months ago, OpenAI claimed that one of their models solved a problem that no human or AI has ever solved before. This ended up being a great embarrassment for OpenAI.

I think it was like alpha proof and alpha geometry that took part in one of the most prestigious mathematical Olympiads in the world and it did very well. I think it was one point away from getting gold if memory serves. But those weren't general reasoning models. Those were very much kind of like the old school AI. They weren't general. They were trained on billions trillions of data points just on that particular thing. in this case coming up with geometric proofs or whatever it was. And then the very next year it was a Gemini based model that came out and took gold. A lot of people I think kind of missed the significance of that because this the the math model that beat the Olympiad wasn't a math model. It was a general model. It was a large language model. So it was just getting good at everything. I think I was getting good at coding and math and probably poetry and giving you the right recipes for pancakes. That's an important thing to realize. If you look at how exceptionally good it's getting at reasoning and that's what these proofs are, what what this math is, it's sort of a testament for how good it's able to reason. I think it's important to kind of like highlight and underline this fact that this isn't some alien thing that's only doing math calculations. This is a general reasoning model. Its general abilities are expanding across the board. These models as we scale them up, as we train them on more compute, more data, various other little algorithmic tricks that we're finding, they're getting better across the board. the stuff that they can do, what they can be applied to is a little bit more universal. And sure, some areas they're much further ahead on than others. But it's important to underline that these headlines, these new discoveries and gold medals aren't being awarded to specific models, bespoke models. They're they're awarded to general reasoning models. So the same models that we're using in Gemini or Chad GBT or Claude, those are the same types of models that are doing this. I think that's an important point. By the way, just like 7 months ago, a lot of people were, you know, not saying very nice things about OpenAI and their, you know, math achievements again because the thing that they posted, the proof already existed somewhere else. So the model just found it somewhere and then said, "I found a proof." And the [snorts] person at OpenAI kind of saw it and was like, "Oh, wow." and they they published it without double-checking. But now it's completely different. All these mathematicians are saying very amazing things about these models. Mitab Sony, one of the the mathematicians that was sent this proof for checking for making sure everything's legit. They said it feels like magic. It's kind of amazing experience to have the machine give back something which really resembles how I work. Alexander Wei was saying how this is making the IMOL excitement from from last year feel kind of quaint. Cheryl Sue is saying that the jury's been out on whether or not these models could do novel research but this result changes that conversation. It would be interesting to see where it kind of goes from here because certainly this is pointing to the fact that yes they can do novel research. Tim Gowowers were saying it's a milestone in AI mathematics and that no previous AI generated proof has come anywhere close to this level. What's really striking to me is that there might be an amazing amount of these cross connections that we had no idea existed because again somebody thought about it and decided to prompt the model like hey look at this can you prove it disprove it and it's like yeah sure here but we had to prompt it we had to bother to check we we had to get that thing going how many different various cross connections or things that could be discovered are just sitting there because no one had bothered to ask or even thought of hey why don't I ask the latest and greatest AI model this specific particular thing by the way if you recall Isomorphic labs they were kind of built they're underneath the alphabet umbrella so Deis Habibus had a lot to do with that so I don't know if he's necessarily like taking active part in it day-to-day but a lot of the things that led up to that were his efforts and alpha fold and all those discoveries. But in a few interviews that I saw with people running isomeorphic labs and with Demis himself, he was talking about how incredibly important these crossdisciplinary connections were. And specifically they were trying to get people from different fields like the best in biology, the best in computer science and then putting them in a room room together to work on those problems which is maybe not a a new idea but in general it does seem like people of different disciplines are kind of like grouped together. You don't have too many true like polymaths that just know a lot of things across a lot of different disciplines. I mean, yeah, they exist, but it's not the most natural thing. We tend to specialize. And if you're a brilliant physicist, you're probably drilling deeper and deeper into that. If you're into biology, you're going deeper and deeper into that. And I think that makes sense for humans. Maybe that is the most sort of effective way for us to just get specialized in our own little niche and learn as much as we can and try to kind of push progress further in that domain. But with AI, maybe hopefully it seems like it is because of this discovery. There might be a way for us to create these connections between disciplines. I mean, it's pretty obvious that math intersects with everything, but maybe so does biology or material science or computer science and physics and medicine and potentially even AI research itself. Interestingly, this might mean that human experts become even more in demand, especially when you combine human experts in different fields. You put them in the room together and you're like, "Okay, use this AI model, try to find some connections between whatever the two of you know and see if we can move something forward. If we can create some novel discovery that's at the intersection of those two or more fields, right?" Then AI proposes something like, "Oh, what if we do this?" Humans can judge. By the way, that's exactly what happened in this scenario, right? We had all these mathematicians kind of judging the output. They've they've approved it and with this human feedback, the AI continues to explore and discover more. At the end of the day, humans decide what matters. And of course, I think this has big implications for automating AI research itself. Alpha Evolve out of Google Deep Mind showed what can be done with a model like Gemini or multiple Gemini models kind of in an ensemble and a harness. That's exactly what Alpha Evolve was. It was Gemini plus a harness. I'm simplifying, but that's kind of what you can think of it as. And it just went to town and discovered some math things that we haven't discovered before. It was able to improve the training of Gemini. like it was able to improve its own training or to improve the next generation of Gemini, how efficient that training went. It improved some of the actual hardware, the chips. It improved Google's Borg center, which is was its data center. It improved how efficiently it conducted its operations. So, with that said, please hit thumbs up if I've helped you connect the dots. And if you don't like puns, then I'm sorry.

items.title: Google entered the "AGENTIC ERA"
items.author: Wes Roth
items.summary: Google just unleashed the era of agents. It teamed up with a chunker of a fund to build [music] out its Neo Cloud.
