items.title: OpenAI Changed Codex, Microsoft Drops 7 NEW Models & Anthropic's IPO Is Coming!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers.
items.transcriptSummary: Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers.

transcript.text: There's a new AI lab in town. Microsoft just launched seven new in-house MAI [music] models at once and says it competes with OpenAI and Anthropic. Anthropic filed confidently first IPO at a valuation of nearly $965 billion and OpenAI turned Codex into a work agent for everyone, not just for developers. So, let's get into it. A few weeks ago, Microsoft told all its employees to stop using cloud models and now we know why because Microsoft just revealed Microsoft AI today and dropped seven new models. Yes, seven new models developed in-house spanning from image, voice, transcription, coding, and reasoning forming the MAI model family, a multimodal ecosystem. So, the first model is MAI thinking one, which is their flagship reasoning, a medium-size model that matches leading models on key software engineering benchmarks and reaches human preference parity with Sonic 4.6 in a blind side-by-side evaluation and is trained from scratch on clean data without distillation, that's their claim. Then there's MAI Code 1 Flash, which is an inference efficient agentic coding model deeply integrated into GitHub Copilot, VS Code, and the Microsoft stack. At 5 billion parameters, it's comparable to Haiku, but cheaper. Then there's MAI Image 2.5 plus a flash variant, which handles both text-to-image and image editing surpassing the arena score of Nano Banana Pro already. Then there's MAI Transcribe 1.5, which they claim is a best-in-world transcription with state-of-the-art accuracy, five times faster than competitors, with domain-specific terminology support across 43 languages. Then there's MAI Voice 2, which is a natural speech generation across 15 languages able to adapt to a voice from a short sample with a lower cost flash variant coming soon as well. Now, when I first saw this, I was thinking that which company is going to be genuinely affected by this and one answer came to my mind that I think a lot of people are going to agree with is Google. Google's whole strategy has been to release a model and then leverage their distribution platform to serve it to an audience. Obviously, they could get away with a sub par model because their distribution for print is so large. Now, a player like Microsoft entering kind of puts pressure on Google. Compared to Open AI and Anthropic, Microsoft has a really strong distribution. As you can see already, these models are deeply integrated into things like GitHub, VS Code, the whole Microsoft stack. So, a lot of people are already going to start working with these models in the background. That has been the whole play for Google and Microsoft, and that is why I think this is going to shake up the whole competitive environment, especially for players like Google. Obviously, these models have just launched, so it's early to tell how competitive they're going to be, but based on what they are saying, they're competitive to Sonic 4.6. So, they're not competing with the latest models at the moment, but this is just a company that was launched a couple of days ago. So, we are going to see more development from Microsoft, and I'm just excited because more competition always means more innovation and more pressure to produce the best quality models. Today was probably one of the most important days for Open AI as a business, but a lot of people are going to miss that because as developers or as people who watch this channel, we're so focused on the consumer side of things. But today, Open AI had a conference called Intelligence at Work, and their pitch was basically that more than 5 million people now use Codex every single week. Originally, Codex started as a tool for software development, but now it's becoming increasingly useful for more kind of work, work that developers are not doing. Think about analysts, marketers, operators, designers. And what's really cool to know is that they make up about 20% of overall Codex users at the moment, but this segment is growing more than three times fast as the developer segment, which kind of shows you how the Codex platform is going to transition into something that is going to be used not only for coding or programming, but going to be used in actual enterprise work. Why is that important for OpenAI as a business? Developers definitely make up majority of the platform at the moment, 80% as they mentioned. But they are not going to be the ones that bring majority of the revenue in the long term. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. If OpenAI wants to continue scaling and continue training models, they need to generate a lot of revenue. Majority of that revenue is going to come from enterprise contracts. Think about this, if you're a small business or even a large enterprise, if you have hundreds of employees, if you sign a contract with OpenAI, majority of the times the pricing works like this. You have a standard fee plus a revenue portion that is tied to the number employees that actually use this. So you can see why this is going to be very important for OpenAI as a business. Now what does this mean for OpenAI? Number one, more and more enterprises and employees are going to get used to their platform. Just imagine, you have somebody who spends 8 to 10 hours working on a specific platform like Codex, and then when he goes home and he does his own personal work, what platform do you think he's going to gravitate towards? Obviously OpenAI's Codex platform. So there's one side of the strategy that most people will miss. Second, as I already mentioned, revenue. Why is that important? I'll get into this later on, but Anthropic just filed for the IPO. What does that mean? The financials and balance sheets are going to become public. If OpenAI wants to file for an IPO, they need to show real revenue on their sheet for their valuation not to plummet. And if they're able to sign a bunch of enterprise contracts, get access to American SMEs, and overall expand internationally, that gives them solid runway not only as a business, but as well as a public company. Now what's really cool is that with this feature there are a couple of important things that I Number one, they launched something called sites. Sites are basically a new kind of canvas for employees ideas. Codex can take your ideas, analysis, and plans and then turn them into dashboards, planners, review workspace, project boards, whatever you want, and you can share that with your employees. As well, there's permission access, so obviously if there's a dashboard that is maybe limited to certain people on your team, unwanted employees won't be able to access it. This is a tool that might not seem big to developers common developers, but this is really important for real work. A lot of times people are creating dashboards, Power BI dashboards, things like that to analyze data, and then share those insights with their respective teams. By making that process much easier and accessible for employees and enterprises, they're going to gravitate towards a tool like that. Second is plugins. Now, there are actually six type of plugins, and they're all targeted to specific type of work. There's a data analytics plugin that helps analysts and business team answer questions with data, and they can explore product and business data, explain why key metrics change, and create reports and dashboard using tools like Snowflake, Databricks, Genie, Hex, and Tableau. There's also a creative production plugin, which helps marketing creative teams turn a brief into assets they can review. Teams can create campaign boards, make and refine display ad variations, and produce product lifestyle shots or e-commerce ready image sets with tools like Figma, Canva, Shutterstock, and more. There's a sales plugin, which helps sales team bring customer contacts into the work that moves deals forward. Sales teams can find high priority accounts and signals, prepare for customer meetings, complete follow-ups, and more. There's a product design plugin, which is built for turning early ideas into prototype teams can review. Teams can explore product directories, audit user flows, things like that. And once again, tools like Figma and Canva will power that. There's a public equity investing plugin that helps investors make sense of market and company information. They can review earnings, compare companies, track signals, and they're going to be using tools like Moody's DataLoop, DataSite, FactSet, and more. And then there's an investment banking plugin, which can help bankers turn research and diligence into client-ready materials. They can prepare pitch materials, analyze comparable companies and transactions, and more. So, you can see OpenAI is heavily targeting six area: data analytics, creative production, product design, sales, and public equity investing, which pretty much cover majority of the corporate environment. You might have very niche stuff that might not be highlighted in the plugins directly, but these plugins probably give you a good head start to start creating and customizing your own plugins. But, what's really important is that we are now entering phase two of AI. Phase one was all about disrupting computer science, software engineering, that type of pipeline. Now, we're entering more into knowledge work area, and this phase two is going to grow pretty fast, as I said, because right now it's going three times faster than the developer community. So, right now Codex might be about 20% of investors and bankers. Maybe in the future we're going to see a split that might surprise a lot of people. And like I mentioned, on June 1st, which was Monday of this week, Anthropic has filed for an IPO. Obviously, this is still the beginning of the pipeline, but what this is going to do is overall give us a better idea of how profitable and how strong these companies are actually are. Obviously, the valuation numbers that we see right now are crazy to see. They're five times the biggest companies in the world right now. Think of Microsoft, these companies are bigger than them. And obviously, because the valuation is taken into account the future growth and projection of these companies. But, when these companies become public, especially Anthropic and OpenAI, we'll get more insight into their actual cost, their revenue structure, what their balance sheet looks like, and that'll give us a better understanding of what their actual valuation is, especially as people keep on using the term AI bubble. By seeing these companies go public, we'll get a better understanding if we are actually in AI bubble, or this is just the beginning of something big that's coming very soon. But that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel, follow our new newsletter as well at universeofai.beehive.com, as well subscribe to the main channel World of AI, and support us on X by following the universeofai.z as well. >> [music] >> Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: MiniMax M3: Rivals Opus, 17x Cheaper, Fully Open!
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal.
items.transcriptSummary: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal.

transcript.text: The MiniMax team is back, and they just dropped M3, which is their first open weight model that combines three frontier capabilities in a single model, coding agentic workflows, and a 1 million token context window. On top of all of that, it's also natively multimodal. And what's really surprising is the price. You're getting state-of-the-art performance, open weights, and it's significantly cheaper than most of the frontier models people use every single day. On a few benchmark, it's actually ahead of Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5, which to be honest, I did not expect from an open model. I'll also get into the demos I ran, but first, let me show you guys what's actually powering this model, because the architecture over here is doing a lot of the work. So, let's get into it. A huge part of this actually comes from MiniMax's sparse attention, which is their new MSA architecture. This is what actually enables the 1 million token context window on M3, with a guaranteed minimum of 512,000. The reason that matters is the way it handles long context. Standard attention has every token look at every other token, so the cost climbs up fast, and the model slows down on big inputs. MSA is sparse. It scores blocks of the content, picks the ones that matter, and only runs attention over those. So, you keep the long window without the speed falling off. That's what makes it strong for long horizon agents, large coding projects, and understanding lengthy videos and documents that most people are doing nowadays. MiniMax M3 is also natively a multimodal model. A lot of models add vision by training a separate image encoder and stitching it onto a finished text model. However, MiniMax trained text and images together from the start, scaling pre-training past 100 trillion tokens, so the image and text representations live in the same space instead of being bridged in at the end. And the reason that shows up in real use is that the model can reason over an image in the same pass as the text around it. In their form filling test, it put every field onto a blank form image at the right coordinates, with the right spacing, and it worked through coordinate math before writing anything. So, for stuff like parsing documents, reading screenshots, or anything layout heavy, it's acting on the structure of the image rather than just describing it. Now, if we look at the benchmarks, specifically the coding and agenting benchmarks, it's strong across the board. On the software engineering bench pro, it sits at 59%, which is past GPT 5.5 and actually Gemini 3.1 pro, and closing in on Opus. On terminal bench 2.1, it sits at 66%, MCP Atlas at 74, and in browser comp at 83.5 sitting above Opus 4.7 79.3. The part that's important to me is that an open weight model is beating Opus and GPT 5.5 outright on a chunk of these. Browser comp, SVG bench, kernel bench hard, and OS world. That hasn't really been the case before. The honest take is obviously these are just benchmarks. How it actually performs in production is what matters at the end of the day. So, I would do some independent testing myself, and you should do it as well to get your own opinion on it. But, the long horizon runs are harder to cherry-pick, so let me show you guys example of what they did with that. So, there are three examples of long horizon work that they showed that are worth talking about. The first is a CUDA kernel one. They handed it empty an FP8 gem kernel to optimize on Hopper GPUs, and they actually didn't even give it working code, just a simple task description and a broken Triton skeleton. And, it worked on it for about 24 hours, ran 147 benchmark submissions, and almost 2,002 calls, and pushed the hardware utilization from 7.6% up to 71.3%, which is about a 9.4 times speed up with nobody touching it. The second is reproducing an ICLR 2025 paper from scratch, or which took about around 12 hours, 18 commits, and replicated the core experiments with the multimodal side reading the charts straight out of the paper, and the long window holding the paper, the code, and the logs all at once. And, the third, which I think is really crazy, is the post-train bench, where the model has to run its own training pipeline, synthesize data, train models, evaluate, iterate, and no humans can be involved. M3 came third on that behind only Opus 4.7 and GPT 5.5. What all three examples are really testing though is long horizon coherence, whether the model can hold its state and recover from its own mistake across thousands of steps instead of derailing, you know, 20 steps in. That's been the actual ceiling on agents being useful in production, and these runs show you that it has improved on a model you can actually host yourself. Now, pricing is the other reason that this is worth paying attention to, and MiniMax is actually sponsoring today's video, so you get an additional 12% discount, but there's a promo running at 50% off, which puts input at 30 cents per million tokens and output at $1.20 for a model going up against GPT 5.5 and Opus. That's well under what they charge. The subscription route is also their token plan, and the thing that's a bit different that there is text, image, speech, and music all drawn from one shared pool, so it covers the whole model family rather than just M3. The $20 tier gets you roughly 1.7 billion M3 tokens a month, and as I mentioned, there's a 12% discount for you guys on every tier. The link is in the description and the pinned comment, and you can actually run this with the standard API, the MiniMax code platform, or you can drop it straight into Cloud Code, Cursor, and CodeX, so wherever you use it. So, it's definitely worth trying out for yourself. So, now I'm showing you guys a couple of generations that the new model generated, and every one of these generations was built by calling the M3 API directly. It's a simple setup, point it at MiniMax's endpoint, add your key, set the model to M3, and it's OpenAI and Anthropic SDK compatible, so just swap the base URL, and I'll link the docs below just in case you guys are confused about how to do it as well. This is the glassmorphism design that I told you to create, and it created a professional dashboard. So, this is not bad at all, like this is obviously a one-shot generation, so based off the prompt I've given it and what it has created is pretty spot on. And then this is the second generation I told you to create, which was a new brutalism style, which is one of the new styles that a lot of people have been testing with the new models because number one, it has a specific style as well as very vibrant and the model has to do a good job of representing that. So, this example is kind of shows you that it's done a pretty good job. It has added animations as well as style formats and everything is coherent. And this was supposed to be for a SaaS-based company, which we can see that it's clearly doing over here. So, this is pretty good. What I really like was like for example, even at the bottom, the small details like this, a tiny CLI for tracking tasks and everything. I think that shows the personality of the new model a little bit, which I like. And then this is kind of like a portfolio page I told it to create, which I think all three of these examples show that when it comes to front-end design, it's pretty good. It's on par with many of the models. Obviously, it didn't generate any images because I didn't ask it to, just a simple template for HTML portfolio website, which is doing a good job of. And as I said, these three examples show the front-end side of the things, but the next couple of generations shows the SVG side of things. For example, this is a whole lot on SVG animation of a sunrise and sunset. So, you can see that the sun just went down, which shows you how capable this model is when it comes to SVG generation. And it has a pretty high ranking on this benchmark as well. And remember, SVG, this is all coded. This is all math and shapes and formats and everything programmed into this. So, this is why it's important to understand and appreciate that because all of this is programmed. So, even if I for example, just to show you guys, if I just minimize this, all of this will stay coherent because it's SVG code generation. Another example is this. Let me just reload this so you guys can see. I told it to create an animated SVG logo for a company called Orbit. And we see it did a pretty great job of doing that. items.title: MiniMax Code: Build Your Own AI Agent Team (Stop Using One!)
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: In 2026, if you're still using one AI agent, you're doing it wrong. You should be now using agent teams.
items.transcriptSummary: In 2026, if you're still using one AI agent, you're doing it wrong. You should be now using agent teams.

transcript.text: In 2026, if you're still using one AI agent, you're doing it wrong. You should be now using agent teams. And with tools like MiniMax Mavis, you can deploy a whole team of AI agents that don't just produce work, they verify it and test it before it gets back to you. You're not spending your day checking AI output for [music] hallucinations, the work comes out done. That's exactly what I'm walking through today, and I'm going to show you guys the workflow, how it's built, and real example of how to use agent teams to produce high-quality work. So, let's get into it. Now, the first question you might have is why do I need an AI agent team? Think about it like this. When you run a real business, you obviously don't hire one person to do everything. You have an HR person, a marketing person, a commerce person, a strategist, and each role is a specialist because no one person is going to do a great job at all of them. The same logic applies to AI. A generalist agent is not going to have the right skills, the right instructions, or the right tools for every job you throw at it. The second you try to make one agent handle everything, you get more hallucination and more confidently wrong answers, and obviously the thing we all hate, more AI slop. The fix for all of this is an AI agent team, a few agents, each one focused on a specific kind of work. And what's really cool is that they all collaborate, work in parallel, they hand off work to each other, but most importantly, they check each other's work. And Mavis is a desktop app that actually lets you run this setup. And it's also multi-agent by default, and it has built-in verification. And what's really cool is that you can add your own specialist whenever you need them. So, when you open up the MiniMax agent application, the first thing you're going to notice is that there's an agent team already built-in, and there are already three agents that you have. You have a coder, a verifier, and a general. So, the general agent is your generalist agent, but you can think of it the one that would be useful for research, writing, planning, summarizing, the stuff that doesn't really need a specialized toolkit. It's your default for anything non-technical. Then the coder agent handles implementation, building scripts, writing apps, reading files, running a test in a sandbox, anything build related will go to your coder agent. Now the verifier is the one that makes Mavis different. It wakes up after the other agents finish with the completely fresh context. It hasn't seen the conversation. It just looks at the final output and runs an independent check on it and it catches what other agents miss. So this is what helps you improve your confidence in your code bases. And quick note before I keep going, today's video is actually sponsored by Minimax, the team behind this whole application and I appreciate them backing this one. I'm only making the video because the architecture is actually worth covering and the demo coming up should make that super obvious. Now the three agents we have here are obviously the starting point. But the real value of Mavis is that you can build your own AI specialist, your own AI agents that specialize in a specific area on top of this whole agent team. To give you guys a concrete example, I run this channel and I need a marketing specialist, specifically an agent analyzes my competition in the AI space. If a video does well, I want to know what made it work, the hook, the pacing, the structure, the language, things like that. And I want it to suggest specific things I can apply to my own videos to make my channel better. So I'll build that agent and all you have to do is click on this create new agent and I'm going to give it a name. I'm going to call this a marketing agent. And the description I've given it is that you analyze YouTube videos in the AI space, identify what made them good and suggest improvements. I wanted to give it an icon as well so I gave it my logo. Now I'm just going to press create and there it is. This is our agent. So now the marketing analyst is now on my team sitting next to the general coder and verifier. And this is the part most people skip. The default agents are basically a baseline, but build the specialist your actual work needs. If you're a lawyer, build a contract viewer. If you're somebody who has a e-commerce business, build a pricing watcher. And the team is supposed to grow with your work so depending on what task you need to accomplish, depending on what you're actually doing, adjust your agent team based off of that. So now I'm going to show you guys how to actually run an agent team whenever you are running a task. So there's a command that you should be familiar with, especially if you're trying to run an agent team, it's called the slash. You would put in the slash command and then put Mavis and then team. So this allows you to run a parallel team, as well allows you to create an agent team or create agents you need. So we'll do this and then you can give it a prompt and then depending on the prompt or the task you have given it, it'll decide how many agents it needs and then launch those agents and get work done in parallel mode. As well, let's say if you wanted to just talk to one agent, you'll go in here and just do single agent and then this forces the agent to only use one agent and you can specify like, "Hey, I want you to use the marketing agent or I want you to use the general agent or coder verifier." So I've done the Mavis team command and one example I want to show you guys is that over here I told that that I wanted to use the Mavis team command and launch a paid AI newsletter on Beehiiv. I need the brand identity, three sample issues. So there's multiple things here that multiple people on your team would do. You would have somebody focusing on brand identity, someone focusing on the writing, things like that. So you can see it created a plan, so I need to do six deliverables across three parallel tracks, research, brand and content and technical and then it came up with the team. So I need to create two specialist agents before launching. items.title: Codex Superapp V2: OpenAI Just UNLOCKED Your Mac! Do you trust it?
items.author: Universe of AI
items.summary: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codex use your Mac while it's locked. Deep Seek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round.
items.transcriptSummary: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codex use your Mac while it's locked. Deep Seek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round.

transcript.text: OpenAI shipped something major that lets Codex use your Mac while it's locked. Deep Seek made a major price cut permanent and is closing a $10 billion round. And Dropbox just dropped their first real update from Project Glass Wing. And Dropbox also quietly made a partnership that's going to change a lot of things. So, let's get into it. OpenAI has a weekly release cadence for Codex now, and they're calling this Codex Thursday. So, four updates went out this week, and the one I want to focus on is locked computer use. So, here's what it does. Codex can now operate on your Mac while your Mac is locked. You send a task from your phone, the laptop handles it, and it stays locked the whole time. Some background on how we got here. Codex already had a computer use feature on macOS. You would install a plugin, give it screen recording and accessibility permissions, and Codex can interact with your apps. It can click on windows, type, navigate menus, use the clipboard, same actions you would do manually. The limitation was that your Mac had to be unlocked and awake, which made remote use kind of pointless. You would basically need to leave your laptop open and logged in for the agent to do anything. This update, however, removes that limitation. There's a new toggle in Codex settings called locked computer use, and when you enable it, Codex installs an Apple authorization plugin that participates in the macOS unlock flow. After that, you can send tasks from the Codex mobile app, and Codex can run them on your locked Mac. The obvious concern is, is this a backdoor into your laptop? OpenAI addresses this directly in the document. They describe locked use as intentionally narrow. It's not a general purpose remote unlock path, and other apps or local processes can't use it to unlock your computer. Only Codex can, and only during what they call active trusted computer use turns. What that means in practice, when a Codex task starts, the system checks whether the unlock attempt is tied to a real in progress computer use turn. If it is, the Mac unlocks. If it isn't, the unlock is denied and you have to log in manually. While the task is running, Codex covers every display. Anyone walking past your desk sees the lock screen overlay, which says Codex is using your Mac. Press any key to take over. They don't see the apps Codex is actually interacting with. The other safeguard is input detection. If Codex sees any keyboard or mouse activity locally, it relocks the Mac immediately and pauses automatic unlock until you log in by hand. Also, touching your computer ends the session. There are limits on what Codex can actually do in this mode. It can handle GUI work, clicking through windows, typing text, navigating menus, copying content. That kind of tasks that don't translate well to a command line. But, it cannot automate terminal apps and it cannot operate Codex itself. And, it cannot interact with system-level admin prompts, the ones that ask for your password or trigger privileged actions. Those still require you to be present. Permissions work app by app. The first time Codex tries to use a new app, you have to approve it. And, you can mark trusted apps as always allowed if you don't want to approve them every single time. One regional note, at launch, lock computer use is not available in the European Economic Area, the UK, or Switzerland. My take on this, the feature itself is useful. Being able to send a task from your phone and have your Mac handle it in the background covers a real gap, especially for GUI-only work that command line agents can't reach. But, what's more interesting is how it's built. OpenAI didn't ship a general remote unlock feature. They built a narrow-scope version with specific constraints. Short-lived authorization, cover displays, relock on local input, manual fallback. The architecture they actually developed kind of seems more like a security spec than a typical product launch that you would expect from Codex. Which is worth understanding because we're going to see more of this type of development in the future. Agents that need persistent access to a user's device will rely on this type of architecture. The way Codex is handling it right now sets a reasonable baseline. Codex also launched three other updates. Number one is app shots, which lets you press both command keys to send the frontmost app window into Codex with a screenshot and the available text. Also, goal mode, which is now out of experimental and available across the Codex app, the ID extension and the CLI. You can set an objective and Codex works towards it for hours or days depending on the task. And there's also advanced annotation mode in the browser, which lets you adjust how a webpage looks while leaving feedback for Codex. So, OpenAI is also asking for suggestions on what people want to see in the next week's drop. So, if you have some valuable suggestions, make sure you guys reply on X to their account and you might see an update that you actually suggested next Thursday. Before we continue, we just launched the Universe of AI newsletter. If you want to stay on top of AI news without having to hunt for it, link is in the description. Don't miss out. We have two updates from Deep Seek today that are worth looking at together because they tell a pretty clear story about how the company is positioning itself. First, the pricing. Deep Seek made their 75% discount on version 4 Pro permanent today. The input with a cash hit is 0.003 per million tokens, cash miss is 0.435, and output is 0.87. After the extended offer ends, those prices will stay officially set to 1/4 of the original price. For context, version 4 Pro is the 1.6 trillion parameter mixture of expert model they released in April. At these prices, it's an order of magnitude cheaper than what you would pay for comparable frontier output from US labs, and they're locking that pricing in. The second piece is a funding round. Bloomberg reported today that Deep Seek is in the final stage of a 70 billion won raise, roughly $10 billion at a pre-money valuation around 45 billion. This is their first time taking outside money. Until now, the lab has been entirely funded by Liang Wang Fung's hedge fund, High Flyer. What Liang told investor in those meeting is the interesting part. He said the company will keep releasing open-source models and that the main goal is pushing the technology forward, not monetization, pursuing AGI, not chasing revenue. Which sounds very similar to what Sam Altman said the goal of OpenAI was back in the day. So, you got a Frontier Lab that just made their flagship model permanently 75% cheaper, raising $10 billion to keep doing more of that. Most of the AI industry is moving the other direction. OpenAI and drop a Google, the pressure is on revenue for them, on enterprise deals, on proving the unit economics. DeepSeek is doing the opposite. They're cutting the price, committing to open weights, and telling investors not to expect commercialization. One thing worth flagging though, the reported backers include the National Artificial Intelligence Industry Investment Fund, that's Beijing's strategic AI vehicle, alongside Tencent, IDG Capital, and Monolith Capital. Liang himself may put in about 20 billion won, so the open-source research lab framing is real, but it's also being partly underwritten by Chinese state capital. The strategic logic here, if you think about it from DeepSeek's side, is that open weights and aggressive pricing are how you build influence at the model layer when you can't compete on compute or distribution with American labs. In short, make it the default, get it embedded everywhere. The Qwen team at Alibaba has been running a similar playbook. Asia's open-source models are spreading globally for exactly this reason. Whether that holds up over time is going to be the question, but $10 billion is a lot. But training version five or whatever comes next is going to cost a meaningful chunk of it. At some point, the math on permanently subsidized pricing has to either work or get rewritten. But for now, the message is consistent. Cheaper, open, research first. Same story that they've been telling since 2025, just with a much bigger balance sheet behind it now. Anthropic published an update on Project Glasswing today for the first time. This is the program they launched last month to secure the world's most critical software before AI models capable of breaking it become widely available. The numbers in this update are worth paying attention to. In about 1 month, Anthropic and roughly 50 partners have used a model called Claude Meitner's preview to find more than 10,000 high or critical severity vulnerabilities across systematically important software. Cloudflare alone found 2,000 bugs, 400 of them high or critical. Mozilla found and fixed 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox 150, which is more than 10 times what they found in the previous version using Claude Opus 4.6. Separately, Anthropic has been scanning over 1,000 open source projects with Meitner's preview, has found about 6,200 high or critical severity vulnerabilities in those alone. After independent triage, about 90% of the reviewed findings turned out to be valid with a 62% rate of being confirmed as actually high or critical. So, that's the scale. What's more interesting is what they said about the bottleneck. Quote from the post, "Progress on software security used to be limited by how quickly we could find new vulnerabilities. Now, it's limited by how quickly we can verify, disclose, and patch the large number of vulnerabilities found by AI." So, that's the big change. Finding bugs used to be the hard part. Now, finding them is fast, and the slow part is humans assessing them, writing patches, and getting users to update. Some open source maintainers have literally asked Anthropic to slow down because they can't keep up. Average time to patch a high critical bug found by Metos preview is about 2 weeks. You can also see the downstream effects elsewhere. Palo Alto Networks shipped five times their usual number of patches in their latest release. Microsoft said the number of patches per patch Tuesday is going to keep trending larger and larger. Oracle is finding and fixing vulnerabilities multiple times faster than before, and there's also a real-world incident in there. One Glasswork partner bank used Metos preview to detect and prevent a fraudulent 1.5 million wire transfer after a threat actor compromised a customer's email and made spoof phone calls. Anthropic obviously hasn't released Metos class models publicly yet. They explicitly said no one, including them, has developed safeguards strong enough yet. Their bet is that by giving their defender side a head start, the ecosystem patches as much as possible before a similarly capable model gets released by someone with a weaker safeguard. For the rest of us, they're making the tooling more accessible. Cloud security is in public beta for enterprise. They say it's been used to patch over 2,100 vulnerabilities in 3 weeks since launch. They're also releasing the skills scanning harness and threat model builder they use internally. The takeaway is AI is now finding critical vulnerabilities at industrial scale. The constraint has moved from offense now to defense. How quickly the patching side adapts is what determines whether this becomes a net positive or a net liability to everyone. Anthropic is expanding its compute deal with SpaceX. Starting in June, they're scaling up to GB200 capacity in Colossus 2. A couple of weeks ago, Anthropic signed a deal to use Colossus 1, the 220,000 GPU supercomputer in Memphis that was urgently built for xAI. SpaceX disclosed the financials in its IPO filing this week and showed that Anthropic is paying 1.25 billion per month through May 2029, nearly 45 billion over the full term, and the expansion announced today as GB200 capacity in Colossus 2, which is the next-generation cluster. For users, this is the more practical part. When Anthropic announced the original Colossus 1 deal, they said directly it would go towards improving capacity for Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers. So, the read here is that this expansion should mean higher rate limits, better availability, and more headroom for training the next generation of models. Tom Brown, Anthropic's Chief Compute Officer, put it as the SpaceX team helping them, quote, find good homes for the Claude's. This partnership is worth monitoring to see if actually things get shipped from this, and if more compute usually shows up as better models a few months later. But, that's it for today's video. Make sure you guys are subscribed to the channel. Follow our new newsletter as well at universeofai.beehive.com, as well subscribe to the main channel World of AI, and support us on X by following the Universe of AI Z as well. Until then, I'll see you guys in the next video.

items.title: Nemotron 3 Ultra NVIDIA's 550B Open Model
items.author: Sam Witteveen
items.summary: Over the past year, the narrative in openweights LLMs has been that Chinese labs are winning. The Kimmy models, Zim models, Minia Max have all had these big impressive models.
items.transcriptSummary: Over the past year, the narrative in openweights LLMs has been that Chinese labs are winning. The Kimmy models, Zim models, Minia Max have all had these big impressive models.

transcript.text: Over the past year, the narrative in openweights LLMs has been that Chinese labs are winning. The Kimmy models, Zim models, Minia Max have all had these big impressive models. But in an attempt to take back that crown, Nvidia has just dropped a 550 billion parameter model that beats many of these trillion parameter models on agent benchmarks. And more importantly, they've published exactly how they did it. If you're building agents, this may just be a gamecher for which open models you should be reaching for. So, naturally, when Nvidia reached out to me to sponsor a video, this is the one that I wanted to get access to. And in this video, I'm going to show you the actual numbers, not just the marketing. We're going to break down some of the recipes of how they've actually made this model, and then we'll have a play with the model to see exactly how this holds up. So Nvidia's been working hard on making a whole suite of open models. So I've covered a few of the Neotron models on the channel. For the Neotron 3 series, they started with the Nano model, which was at the end of last year. And the Nano model was all about being a small, fast, high efficiency kind of model. Earlier this year, they released Neotron 3 Super, and that was really built for multi-agent applications. So, that one really took off with things like open claw and stuff like that. And that brings us to today where they're rolling out Neotron 3 Ultra. Now, Neotron 3 Ultra is their most capable model yet. It's the flagship model that they've been working on for a while. This is not a small model. It's probably not a model that you can even run locally, but it certainly is a model that companies will be able to use on prem, be able to fine-tune for their use tasks, that kind of thing. So, Neimatron 3 Ultra is certainly a beast of a model. We're talking about a model that's 550 billion parameters, mixture of experts model with 55 billion parameters active. And the goal with this is that it's been trained again for agentic users. So, this model is made not to be any sort of chatbot. This is Benentic model where it can write, it can use tools, it can code, and it can do long horizon many step actions just like we've seen with some of the Chinese open models like the Kim K2.6 etc. You can really think of Neotron Ultra as Nvidia's attempt at making one of these huge models that while it is really big is trying to get on par with some of the Frontier Labs and some of the performance of models like Anthropic Opus, the recent GPT series, the sort of Gemini Pro kind of models. Now, for me again, what sets this apart, just like I talked about recently in the Nimitron Omni video, is that it's not just the model. It's really interesting thing here is the fact that they're actually openly talking about how it was made. The fact that they've released data sets, the fact that it looks like we've got recipes for what they actually did here means that if you do want to take this model and fine-tune it for a very specific task for an organization, and I should stress that nowadays more and more what I'm seeing is big organizations taking some of these open models, often the Chinese open weights models, but also things like Gemma 4 and the Neotron models, and then basically fine-tuning them to have custom versions that are really good for the company's specific use tasks to replace proprietary models, whether that's from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc. If you listen to the Venturebe podcast that I do recently, we've had people on from LinkedIn and from Pinterest, from a number of these different really big companies talking about doing exactly that where they're taking these open weights models, customizing them for their very specific use cases and then serving them at scale for millions if not hundreds of millions of users. I think for LinkedIn it's over a billion users for what they've been doing with some of the small open weights models. So one of the techniques that Nvidia has used to create Neotron 3 Ultra is exactly one of the techniques that LinkedIn told us about on the podcast and this is this whole idea of multi-teer on policy distillation and the idea here is that you take a very strong base model. So they train up like the Neotron base model they then train one version of it for code. They train one version for tool use, one version for instruction following and stuff like that. And then what they do is they distill from each of these teachers that they've trained up to be really good at specific things down to one final model. So the advantage there is that you're getting basically these strong teachers that are very strong at what they've been sort of taught to be strong at, but then you're able to use those to distill them down to the final model that is able to be good at all of these things. Now what actually happens there is it turns out that by training the separate teachers and then distilling down you actually get a model that's much stronger than just taking the one model and training it on all of those things. And one of the cool things here is that this can be done in sort of an RL fashion where once you've basically got that teacher it can actually sort of coax this out of the base model as it goes along. Now, another thing that Nvidia has focused on with Neotron 3 Ultra is this whole idea of post-training for agent harnesses. So, the idea here is that as agent harnesses, whether that's open claw, whether that's Hermes, whether that's some kind of lang deep agents, etc., they create these trajectories. And if you train the model on those trajectories and train them specifically for task completion of these, so you give them things like where perhaps they're going to get things wrong. They learn to be able to backtrack. They learn to be able to fix errors by using the harness, by using the different tool calls, by using perhaps some kind of memory, etc. This just allows the model to be much much stronger at these particular kind of agentic tasks. Now, another thing is that this is often done in some kind of RL environments. So, one of the cool things that Nvidia has been talking about is actually opening up a lot of these data sets and the actual RL environments. So, we know for example that RL environments were the things that sort of made the Kimmy 2.5, the Miniax 2.7 models, you know, the GLM models around that time all have this sort of liftoff where they suddenly got a lot better. that was coming because of the RL environments with Nvidia opening up a lot of these R environments. And I've got to stress that as I'm recording this, I haven't had a chance to play with all of those. That's something that maybe I will look at in the future. But with them opening this up, people are actually going to be able to see how these things are being made and how these things are actually being used for different kinds of post-training on a large model like this. And usually that kind of thing is something that you know the different model companies don't want to make public. From what I've read so far, Nvidia plans on releasing a lot of these different environments, which is definitely a huge win for the open source community. Even if you don't end up using this model, there is a good chance that you will end up using a smaller version of some model that was trained on some of these data sets and with some of these RL environments. I should also point out that the model itself actually is like a hybrid mixture of experts architecture. It's actually supporting things like multi-token prediction. It's got a million context window. And while you're probably going to need something like, you know, multiple H100s or a B200 to be able to actually run this, you are certainly going to see this being served by a lot of the inference companies that are out there. and my guess is they'll see them making it available for fine-tuning etc. Okay, so when we come and look at the benchmarks for this, you can see that Neotron 3 Ultra is doing very well for a lot of the different tasks. Now, that doesn't mean that it's actually winning for all the tasks in here, but look at this. This is a 550 billion parameter model. It's comparing to models that are actually much bigger than it. The Quen 3.5 model, it easily beats. It would have been nice to see it being compared to some of the newer Quen models which have also had a lot more RL environment training. And I think that's one of the key things that's going on here. If you look at the GLM 5.1 which I covered before, this model was heavily post-trained on these RL environments. And even then, Neimatron Ultra is doing better than the one trillion parameter model. It does make me wonder if someone actually just takes this and runs it on a lot more of these RL environments, would we see a substantial bump in the actual results here? Now, of course, the biggest advantage of being smaller than the other models means that you're going to be faster than the other models. You can see here, this is from artificial analysis team where their 3 ultra is getting over 300 tokens per second, much faster than the Kimmy and the GLM models. Finally, if we come in and look at Pinchbench, so this is a benchmark that's basically geared towards agent harnesses like Open Claw. You can see that while Anthropic is best overall, the best openweights model here actually is this Nvidia Neotron 3 Ultra. And you can see coming in and looking at this that the Ultra model really isn't that far behind the proprietary models like the Claude Opus 4.8 in here. So knowing that you're getting a much cheaper model here, this definitely makes sense that Nvidia is positioning this as a model that people can use for running their agents both cheaply and efficiently. So let's jump into a notebook and have a look at how this actually works for reasoning and tool calling. Okay, so I'll quickly just go through a quick demo of using this. And as always, I'll put the notebook in the description. You can have a play with it yourself. So, I'm actually running this from Nvidia's cloud. So, basically, you just need to set up an account and put in your uh Nvidia API key in there, but you certainly don't need to run it here. You'll find this model on Open Router. You'll find it on a lot of the different inference providers. So, really pick the one that you're most comfortable with. I would say there. All right. We've just got then some streaming and some helper functions in here for just doing this. We also want to set up some tools for basically testing things out. So we've got a calculator tool. We've got kind of like a little rag tool that basically we can ask it about GPU specs since this is in and it will basically return back a bunch of information there. And you'll notice that everything that we're doing for sort of tool definitions and stuff like that is in the open AI uh sort of format and the model's been built so that it can use an open AI API endpoint, you know, very simply. All right, the key things here is this is a reasoning model, but you've got different options with the reasoning. So you can see here that we can basically just pass in enable thinking equals true. And you will see in this case we've got our helper function printing out the thinking in green. You can see that okay, we've got all the thinking for this simple test and then it comes to the answer and it just basically prints out the answer there. Now you've also got the ability to be able to set the level of thinking that you want. So this can be low effort of where you've basically just got short reasoning. This is for when you want a low cost, a low latency and stuff like that. You've then got default thinking which the model itself will actually decide how long the chain of thought is going to be. And they've got a reasoning budget. So you can actually explicitly sort of tell it, okay, this is the number of thinking tokens that I want you to sort of max out at. Now, even though you can see here, I've set that to 16,000. In my testing, very rarely it will even get close to that kind of budget here, it does seem to be very succinct in its reasoning. So you can see here if we look at the outputs here we've got the loweffort one where we've got a certain amount of reasoning and then we've got the answer coming out the default one we've got a certain amount of reasoning and then the answer out and then we've got our longer one. Now if we actually look at these we can see that okay the approximate amount of reasoning tokens yes went up but the interesting thing here is that the default one in this case the default one's actually longer than the sort of budget one that we set here. So I've given it another one here that's not a mathematical kind of thing. It's much more sort of you know a thinking thing. And you can see that sure enough on this one we had the loweffort be only about 100 tokens. the default one a little bit under 200 and then the budgeted one where we've said you know go for a long amount of time it's still only going to sort of 200 tokens. So my testing this kind of fits for when you want a model just to basically get to the point and give you an answer back very quickly. All right, we can do the same thing with sort of tool calls. You can see here we're basically getting it some tools. These were the tools that we defined earlier on. So, we've got some tools about GPU specs and how much power they're using. We're asking it to basically calculate this out. So, we've passed in a calculator. We've passed in the GPU spec. You can see here that it's done a pretty good job at deciding, okay, this is the tool that it's going to use. This is the arguments for that tool. Then, we get the response back. In round two, it's going to basically use the calculator tool. it's going to decide, okay, what it's going to actually put into that calculator tool. And then finally, in round three, it's basically just going through, not using any tools. It's just giving the us the answer back. And you can see it does a quite a nice job at that. So the model is certainly good at doing these agentic runs of where you want it to basically run a bunch of tools, be able to use those outputs, process them, and to be able to work out what the next tool would be that makes sense for this particular agent use case. So overall, this is definitely what you want for things like your open claw, nano claw, your Hermes agent, that kind of thing where you've got an agent that you're injecting lots of tools in. you want to be able to get, you know, the various responses out, etc. So, overall, I would say check the model out. This is certainly a model that, while I necessarily wouldn't use it as my top tier model for everything, if you want to run a sort of personal agent, uh, single tenant user agent, this is definitely a good model for that. And it makes a lot of sense to do this because it's both very quick, it's reliable, it's got the reasoning that you need, but it's not going to break the bank like some of the Frontier models. And like I said earlier on in the video, for me, probably the biggest thing about this is not just the model itself. It's that Nvidia is making these training recipes available. They're making data available. They're making it possible for lots of companies to not only see how this was done, but to be able to take this and work on their own versions, which I think is a huge win for open weights models and for open LLMs in general. So, as always, let me know in the comments what you think, if you've got any questions or anything. Found the video useful, please click like and subscribe, and I will talk to you in the next video. Bye for now.

items.title: Cosmos 3 - NVIDIA's World Foundation Model
items.author: Sam Witteveen
items.summary: Okay, one of the areas that I think is most interesting that's going on in AI at the moment is actually all around physical AI. So, while mostly I've tended to talk about language models and stuff like that here, really where I see things actually going a lot now is actually along the path of building world models.
items.transcriptSummary: Okay, one of the areas that I think is most interesting that's going on in AI at the moment is actually all around physical AI. So, while mostly I've tended to talk about language models and stuff like that here, really where I see things actually going a lot now is actually along the path of building world models.

transcript.text: Okay, one of the areas that I think is most interesting that's going on in AI at the moment is actually all around physical AI. So, while mostly I've tended to talk about language models and stuff like that here, really where I see things actually going a lot now is actually along the path of building world models. And a good example of this is Cosmos 3, which was announced by Nvidia today at the GTC Taipei conference. And in many ways, Cosmos 3 is a whole step forward in creating a world foundation model. So, this is an omni model. It's multimodal. It can do five different modes. It can do text, images, videos, and actions for things like robotics. And not only can it take those five modalities in, but it can actually generate those five modalities out. And in the past to do this, basically people would do something like take a multiple models and stitch them together to do this. So, you might have a vision language model. You might have some kind of action model. You might have some kind of video diffusion or image generation model in there as well. What Cosmos 3 has done is basically collapse all of these into one model. So, it's important to understand that this is both understanding where it's basically taking those inputs in, and then it's got a generation part where it can actually generate out in those modalities. So, if you think about this, to get this model to be able to do end-to-end of any of those five modalities in and any of those five modalities out is a very impressive task. So, how do they actually do this? Well, if we look at the actual architecture in here, we can see that they're using what they're calling a mixture of transformers. So, this is a dual tower. So, you can see here they've got an autoregressive side. Now, this is what they call the reasoner. And this is for basically taking all the inputs in. And then they've got a second tower, which is the diffusion side, which basically is the generation tower. And that basically allows them to generate the different kinds of outputs needed, whether that's going to be things like generate a whole new video for creating synthetic data, perhaps video plus audio, whether that's going to be generating images, whether that's going to be generating actions that can be used for robotics, et cetera. And so Cosmos 3 is a huge step forward in this direction. In the previous versions of Cosmos, they tended to section these off and make different models for doing this. So you had like a predict model, a transfer model that could do certain things. Here though, they're really focused on three main models here. So you've got Cosmos 3 Super, which is be 32B model * 2, because you've got the two different towers going on there. You've got Cosmos 3 Nano, which is an 8 billion parameter side per tower. So playing around with it, looks like that's about 16 billion parameters in total. And then the one that they haven't actually released yet, but does sound very interesting, is an edge version of this, which you would be able to run on device, real-time, local hardware, that kind of thing. Now, I've been playing with the Cosmos 3 Nano, and it's pretty impressive what this thing can actually do. So for a while now, one of the key things of the Cosmos models has been the ability to actually scale up creating synthetic data. And Cosmos 3 really takes this to the next level. So I'll show you a video later on that I made using this model to do inference for creating video data to basically train up a robot arm or robot arms to actually pick things up. Now, the use cases for this are going to be many. You can imagine pretty much every robotics startup is going to be playing with this kind of model to both be able to create synthetic data for them to learn on, but also create data which can then predict out next actions and stuff. Don't forget, this is not just a random video generator. This has been trained to actually be grounded on being able to go from images to actions and videos to actions. Now, along with the open weights that they've actually released here, Nvidia has also released a very substantial technical report that not only goes through the model architectures, but it also shows where they used other models off the shelf to be able to incorporate in this. So, they've made strong use of the Kwenta 3VL models, the 8B for the Cosmos 3 Nano, and the 32B for the Super, but they've also been able to reuse the 1 2.2 VAEs in here, and I think other elements for the audio and image encoders and stuff in here. Now, it is really interesting how they've come up with this two-tower mixture of transformers architecture where they've got the auto-regressive on one side, and they've got a diffusion model on the other side, and how these are able to share multimodal attention across the two. Another thing that I think is really useful about the Nvidia papers recently has been their whole sort of breakdown of what data they're using for pre-training uh for this kind of thing, and then also what data they're using for supervised fine-tuning. And we can see that in here for the reasoner data where we can see, "Okay, what they've actually used for pre-training and then what they've used for SFT." I think that becomes a really good guide if you want to basically take one of these models and fine-tune it to for a very specific use case that you want to do. If you've already built up some real-world data for training something with physical AI, this model now allows you to basically expand that massively. And you can see that here in some of the examples where they're basically rendering things out with semantic segmentation, but they're also then able to render out full color videos. All right, so I've been basically testing this out doing inference with it on a DGX Spark, and you basically pass in a JSON file of what it is that you want to generate. So, in this case, you can see I've got the model mode being text to video, and then you can see the prompt here is all about basically a wooden display cabinet with various fruits including bananas, apples, pears, oranges, and then the robotic arms are basically positioned at the bottom to actually go through and pick these things up. So, you can see for basically just text to video here, this is actually a pretty good rendering out. Now, it doesn't seem to be perfect that occasionally it seems to lose consistency perhaps when it drops the objects, but we also could have rendered out text to image. We could also render out text to a video with audio, image to video, video to video where we can actually have it change the video as it's going on, and then we can also have it do things like forward dynamics where it predicts the next state given a particular action. So, you could imagine you can feed in something where perhaps you've got some kind of representation along with an action and then have it predict out what would actually happen based on that action. So, overall, this is not going to be the most common kind of model that people are going to be using, but certainly you can make the argument that if we're on the path to AGI, these kinds of world models are going to be a huge signal as to how far on that path we are. It really shows how this learns a different kind of intelligence that does relate to the physical world much more than any of the language models alone learn. So, I put the links in the description. You can go and check it out yourself and actually see how these things are actually developing. And if you are interested in doing anything with robotics or physical AI, these models are going to be crucial to what you're actually doing. So, let me know in the comments what your thoughts are about this kind of thing. As always, if you found the video useful, please click like and subscribe, and I will talk to you in the next video. Bye for now.

items.title: Running Local AI on AMD
items.author: Sam Witteveen
items.summary: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge.
items.transcriptSummary: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge.

transcript.text: Okay, so I think it's becoming very clear that local AI is a huge part of the AI future. Open weight models have closed the gap to the frontier models from being really far behind to being somewhere between sort of three to six months off the cutting edge. That gap is shrinking, not growing. Meanwhile, if you look at the frontier labs, tokens look like they're getting cheaper on paper, but practically people's bills are going up because agents and reasoning eat tokens at a completely different scale than chat ever did. And then the other thing that's becoming totally clear is that people really want to have privacy and control over the AI that they're using. And so while there are these amazing frontier models that are coming, the big question seems to be is who's going to be able to afford to use them at scale. And more and more local AI is just starting to make more sense for a lot of the workloads out there. So the question I've been looking at more and more lately is what actual hardware stacks can get you there. And in this video I want to take a look at the AMD side of that. So I've teamed up with Zidex and AMD to get access to one of their workstations with a Threadripper 9980X and an AMD Radeon AI Pro R9 700 GPU with 32 gig of VRAM. I got to say at the start the results I'm seeing are really impressive and have actually surprised me on what can actually be done now with this kind of system. All right, so let me unpack again why local. The open weight models that we're seeing now, the Qwen 3.6, the Gemma, the Kimmy, the Deep Seek models, etc. These aren't toys. You can do real work with them. I've been covering many of them on my channel and if you've been following the open space at all, you can feel that this trend is not slowing down. While it's clear that the top three frontier labs do have an advantage, Open models have caught up and surpassed a lot of the second-tier labs that are still trying to position themselves at the frontier. But, the biggest factor that I'm seeing is tokens. And this falls into sort of two parts. The first part is that as these models get so good, people want to be able to run them on their own machine doing things for things like coding agents, for personal agents like Open Claw, like Hermes agent, etc. And those particular tasks because they're using reasoning and because they're using agentic calls, they are burning through tokens. If you're paying for a frontier lab model for that, you're going to find that you better have a decent budget. The days of the all-you-can-eat plans are are being dramatically scaled back so that they're only supporting their own particular coding agents, etc. And as we're seeing the next generation of models being talked about, it's pretty clear that those are going to cost a lot more than what the current top-tier models are going to cost. So, this combined with wanting to basically control your data means that you have to look at local AI solutions. All right. So, what I'm going to do in this video is I want to show you how the AMD system that I've been using is working and show you what's actually possible as we're sort of coming up to the middle of 2026. If you want to look at something like this to be your AI workhorse computer. All right. So, the hardware itself here is definitely impressive. Every component, both the GPU and the CPU, are AMD here. So, the CPU that I'm using is a Ryzen Threadripper 9980X. This is definitely a powerful core of the overall system. The GPU is the Radeon AI Pro uh 9700 with 32 gigs of VRAM. It's really interesting to see how this actually performs on the modern AI stack. So, we're going to look at some things like Ollama and LM Studio for running LLMs. We'll also look at some image generation and stuff like that. But also I want to dive into code and showing you actually running things like the Transformers library, Unsloth for doing some fine-tuning, and even just running some straight-up PyTorch on this machine. So let's jump in and have a look at it. Okay, so whenever I get a new GPU or a new system like this, the first thing I want to check out is the LLM stack. And generally that's going to start before I go into code with just simply Ollama and LM Studio. So that's one of the things I find people are running every day, and I find that you can kind of tell from the performance on that how things are actually going to go. So let's start with LM Studio. The setup is actually pretty smooth here. LM Studio now ships with a ROCm runtime. So you can point it at the runtime, restart, and it just sees your card. Now the cool thing with this is using the 32 GB card here, I really don't have to make huge compromises on the quantizations. In fact, all of the models that I'm installing, I'm literally just installing the quant that they basically recommend. Usually that's going to be 4-bit. If it's a smaller model, I can actually usually go for something that's 8-bit. Or if it's something like the Gemma 4 4B model, etc., I can go full resolution here. And you'll see that running this, like this is the Qwen 3.6 mixture of experts here, and I'm able to get very good token response rates out of this. It's able to return things back really quickly, and you can see that I'm averaging around about 160 tokens per second on this model, which is not only way faster than you can actually read, it's also a good speed for using with agents. So already this means that I've got access to a really cool model that has got full reasoning on and off abilities that I can basically turn the reasoning on or off. I can then use it for vision if I wanted to with this particular model. I can then even do things like bring in a document, have it load up, have it be able to set this up and go on the fly. And so I've got now the whole sort of chat with text going on. I'm getting out simple answers. And again, of course, the cool thing here is that this is running fully locally. And you can see at any point, if I want to, I can actually change the context window size in here. So, okay, you can see here I'm using around 64K, but I can come out and make that longer. And I'm still getting good speed from this model. And you can do all the same things in Ollama as well. In fact, lately with Ollama, they're also going down the road of Open Claw and stuff like that. And you can actually run this whole thing locally with one of the models that has been optimized to do those kind of agent tasks in here. So, for running LLMs out of the box, you're going to be able to run a lot of high-quality models the moment that you're finished downloading them in here. All right, so now it's probably a good time to explain a little bit of what is going on underneath LM Studio and Ollama. And I'm actually talking about the layer that they sit on. So, this is the Radeon Open Compute Platform, also just commonly known as ROCm. And honestly, this is the thing that 10 years ago when I was building a deep learning computer, made a lot of people kind of nervous in here. Back then, people would be seriously impressed by the AMD hardware, but the software compatibility was where the issue it was. Now, I'm very happy to report this is just not an issue today. Pretty much all the deep learning frameworks support ROCm and allow you to basically run and train models, not just inference, but being able to train models as well, on these cards like the Radeon 9700. In fact, as I was initially setting this up, LM Studio ran out of the box, Ollama ran out of the box, and even things like PyTorch has official ROCm wheels. Meaning that you can just go to the install selector, pick ROCm, and then copy the pip command, and it installs. And the cool thing is that once you've got that installed, your existing PyTorch code, etc., mostly just runs fine out of the box. Things like the Transformers library and a lot of the other common sort of frameworks that people use with PyTorch are now fully compatible with ROCm. And AMD has clearly put a lot of work into both ROCm and HIP, their translation layer, to basically make all of this work. And like I mentioned, this is not just for inference. You can actually do full sort of fine-tuning and even training from scratch on this. In fact, Unsloth themselves have put out a guide to using Unsloth for fine-tuning LLMs on AMD GPUs. And generally, you'll see as long as you're using that ROCm-optimized version of PyTorch, you're not going to really run into a lot of issues for doing standard kind of workloads. This is going to work pretty much just straight out of the box. And the cool thing is if you do need to go deeper into ROCm, they've got a whole bunch of documentation in here, and you can see that just looking at the date, this is being updated quite often, and it's clearly a priority for AMD to make these GPUs work for the AI workloads people are doing. All right, another thing that people want to do often is to basically make generative images and generative videos. So, probably the best way to do this nowadays is with ComfyUI. And if you actually come into install ComfyUI, you can actually choose a ROCm version there, which will allow you to run this with a GPU, etc., that I've got going on here. You can see that very quickly, I can start to generate a bunch of different images to try out different things here. I can even take something like this. I can see, okay, I've got this nice picture of a cat. I want to see how much just changing the seed is going to affect this. And then I can just generate again, and you can see that it's generating pretty quickly for me to be able to actually see the difference. So, we can see that just changing the seed really hasn't changed the cat too much, but it's changed the angle that the cat is actually on there. So, this is not just for doing images here. You can pick a bunch of different things in here. You can basically go for a text-to-image kinds of things. You can also do image-to-image stuff. You can go for models that generate video in here. This is something that you can certainly try out. I can see a lot of these models are quite well known already of things like the LTX2 models, the Waifu 2.2 models. I think the LTX 2.3 is in here somewhere as well. But, the cool thing with this is once I've downloaded the weights, I can actually just set it to generate a bunch of different examples to look and see, okay, what it is it that I actually want to generate out. They've also got a limited number of audio models, and then image-to-3D models, etc. And of course, I can find other models to download. These are just some of the popular ones in here. So again, the AMD machine and the GPU are actually performing well here, allowing us to basically run ComfyUI, run decent-sized models. So, this is something that I'm finding the system actually works really well on here is being able to generate this kind of things. Okay, so for me, where ROCm really shines is when you actually install Linux on your machine. So, of course, you can have WSL on Windows to basically have Linux, but if you make a dual-boot system where you've actually got Linux, you can actually then get full support from things like PyTorch, etc. So, you can see here that basically I've got a Linux machine, and I've got ROCm 7.2 running here. And you'll see that I just can't do that with Windows. When I select Windows, I can't basically select this. And you'll see that once I've got that installed, I can actually import torch and I can actually see that okay, I've got a full replacement for CUDA in here. So, you can see here okay, that this is available. I get the device name, which is the Radeon graphics and stuff like that. And then once I've got that set up, I've now given PyTorch access to the GPU. So, I can actually come along and you can see here I'm training a model just a simple CIFAR-10 model where it's fully running the GPU. And you can see it's basically trained a simple ResNet model on CIFAR-10 there. And I've actually set it up so that we've got a Gradio interface here with a small ResNet. And you can see that if I click on one of these, I can see okay, what's the prediction there. I can shuffle some examples. And it looks like it's doing reasonably well at least on those examples. And you can see here that now I'm basically just printing out one of the files where I'm loading one of the Gemma 4 models, running it with transformers locally. And if I want to run the actual Gemma model with a Gradio interface here, you can see it's basically got the device where it's actually running. It's loaded my model. And then I can just chat to it in here. And you can see that sure enough, it's going through generating very quickly with the transformers library in here. It's set to a limited number of tokens out. But you can see that okay, this is generating quite well here. We can actually change the tokens out. So, this is just doing it with the purely transformers library. If we wanted to, we could also do it with vLLM and serve things out of there. So, the cool thing about having the Linux environment here is that we've got multiple ways that we could do this now. We don't have to just do it in something like LM Studio. This This using the full resolution version of the Gemma 4 model here.
